bibl.author: Falconer, Edmund
bibl.title: Extremes; Or, Men of the Day
A Comedy in three acts
Lacy's Acting Edition, volume 42, No. 0616

revisionDesc.change: Add wikidata link to titleStmt where possible
Completed the bits Claude missed; hand checked against VPP page images
TEI XML structure added by Claude (up to a point)

titlePage.byline: By EDMUND FALCONER. MEMBER OF THE DRAMATIC AUTHORS' SOCIETY, Author of “The Cagot,” “A Husband for an Hour,” “Too Much for Good Nature” “Husbands Beware,” &c, &c.
titlePage.docImprint: THOMAS HAILES LACY, 89, STRAND, (Opposite Southampton Street, Covent Garden Market), LONDON.

div.p: TO HIS LANCASHIRE FRIENDS, WHOSE SOMETIMES EXTREME BLUNTNESS OF SPEECH, HE ALMOST INVARIABLY FOUND NEIGHBOURED BY EXTREME KINDNESS IN ACT, THE AUTHOR, IN A “GRADELY” SPIRIT, DEDICATES THIS COMEDY.

div.head: PREFACE.
div.p: THE materials for so long a Comedy as Extremes unexpectedly proved, must, of course, have been previously acquired; but no particular consciousness of their existence preceded the design and composition of the Drama, which, from conception to completion, as it is now presented, occupied the Author a period of sixteen days. Although certain, that of mere haste of production it must have many faults, its success upon the stage has made him fearful of attempting to amend, that, which no longer seems his own work. When the fire of composition has been suffered to die out, as it generally is once accepted work passes the door of the studio, judgment, no longer warmed by the spirit of conception, may be potent to criticise, but is feeble to improve; and so, as an exercise to the always vigorous judgment of the critics, yet not without an avowed dependance on their mercy, the Author leaves the faults of the Comedy of Extremes.
div.note: Note.—For various reasons the Author cannot particularize, and therefore begs the Ladies and Gentlemen named in the original cast generally to accept his sincere thanks.

performance.p: First performed at the Lyceum Theatre, August 26th, 1858.

list.item: Sir Lionel.—Fashionable morning dress for summer, dark hair, whiskers, and moustache. Act II.—Fashionable morning dress for October, hat, and gloves. Act III.—Full dress.
Adolphus.—Same, only a little more extravagant; light wig divided in c, light beard, and long-peaked moustaches falling down.
Dr. Playfair.—Plain suit of black all through; low crowned hat with broad brim turned up; iron-grey wig slightly bald.
Mr. Cunningham.—Black suit with over frock coat; white hair.
Everhard Digby.—Same style as Sir Lionel, but plainer; no moustache.

Robin.— Green cutaway coat with canary-coloured waistcoat, and light cord trowsers, wide-awake hat, light hair. Act II.—Grey suit, peg-top trowsers. Act III.—Blue frock, velvet collar, snuff-coloured trowsers, and white waistcoat.
James.—Smart modern livery; coat buttoned, large bouquet in breast, hair powdered.
Frank.—Full dress suit of black. Act II.—Frock coat, grey trowsers and waistcoat. Act III.—Morning winter suit; then fashionable wedding dress.
Mrs. Vavasour.—Fashionable morning dress. Act II,—Walking dress. Act III.—Full dress for old lady.
Lucy.—Morning dress, hat. Act II,—Morning dress; riding dress. Act III.—Morning dress, And last Scene—Bridal dress, wreath.
Miss Euphemia.—Morning dress, different for each Act.
Mrs. Wildbriar.—Handsome plaid silk and cap. 2nd—Claret-coloured silk velvet, turban to match. 3rd—Light moire antique. 4th—Light green silk.
Jenny.—White muslin, 2nd—Fashionable silk, hat and feather. 3rd— Handsome morning dress; wedding dress without wreath.
Jemima.—Plain coloured muslin, small apron, and cap.

body.head: EXTREMES; OR, MEN OF THE DAY.

div.head: Scene First.
div.stage: A richly-furnished Drawing Room, with large bay window at the back, opening upon a terrace, and affording view of lawn fringed with flowers; extensive woods beyond; an entrance to conservatory, R. U. E.; arched entrance to a library, within which statues and paintings are visible, L. U. E.; doors of entrance, R. and L.; books, writing materials, newspapers, vases, &c. on tables; sofas, screen, chairs, &c.; a portrait of Lucy Vavasour, L. 2 E.
James discovered reading Times newspaper, L. C.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: “The bank having noted a further reduction in its rate of discount, the funds rapidly rose from 93 1/8 to 95 1/4; and finally Three per Cent. Consols were quoted at 95 3/8. New Three per Cents, and Reduced Annuities rose in an equal ratio. Business on ‘Change yesterday was exceedingly brisk, and confidence seemed completely restored in the money market.” That’s a comfort; while that ere monetary crisis lasted, and the funds kept a bobbing up and down, and hevery day brought hintelligence of a fresh smash, it was really hawful for people who ‘ad a little money, to think of what was a going to ‘appen.

div.stage: Sofa. Screen. Chair. Window. Chairs. Chair. Chair. Small Table. Table. Chair. Chair. Chair. Table. Chair. Chair. Small Table. Chair.

sp.speaker: Dr. Playfair.
sp.p: (outside, L.) Here, some one!—is there nobody in the house? I object to being left alone.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: That is Dr. Playfair, one of the trustees and executors; and as he objects to everything, he might object to my reading the news. (puts paper on table)

div.stage: Enter Dr. Playfair, L. 2 E.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Has the solicitor—I mean Mr. Cunningham—arrived?

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: (R.) I {h}am {h}under the {h}impression, sir, that he has not; but it is possible that I might be mistook.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: (L.) I object to that indirect and circumlocutory method of answering a question. Why can’t you say that you don’t know—short and straightforward? When a man’s ignorant of any matter, nothing like owning it off hand.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: I never confesses total {h}ignorance upon any subject.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Then the greater humbug you. Do you know where the Times paper is?

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Yes, sir; it is at this present moment deposited on the table to the {h}extreme left of the apartment.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: I object—but there’s no use in objecting to noodleism when you are talking to a noodle.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Shall I do myself the felicity of obtaining and presenting it to you?

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
James.
sp.p: If it wasn’t that he’s {h}executor, and might {h}offer {h}obstructions to my receipt of a legacy, if there should be one, I should tell him that he was no gentleman. I have been in the service of a dook and a marquis, and never was ordered {h}out of a room before. Oh, Jeames Dodsworth, this comes as a punishment on your {h}avarice, and for demeaning yourself to take service with commoners, merely because they give larger salaries, and {h}allows more perquisites. The {h}aristocracy may be poor now and then; but they {h}always {h}appreciates sentiment, {h}elegance of diction, and refinement of manners.

div.stage: Enter EVERARD DIGBY, R.

sp.speaker: Everard.
sp.p: Ah, my dear doctor! I am most happy to see you.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
Everard.
sp.p: Ha, ha! always the same contentious, but sensible adviser. I am glad to see you in the positive degree, and comparatively more glad to see you than ——

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: A dun or a bailiff—no compliment that. I like it the better, and accept it frankly. (shakes hands) What brings you here? Don’t expect a legacy, I hope? I’m quite sure that you’re not mentioned.

sp.speaker: Everard.
sp.p: Oh, I never had the slightest reason to indulge an expectation. You know that I am a lawyer, and being cousin to Miss Vavasour, she and her mother have invited me to pass the long vacation here. It’s partly a friendly, partly a professional visit; they may want my advice as to the settlements, after the will has been read.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Oh, she has made up her mind to inherit?

sp.speaker: Everard.
sp.p: Can there be a doubt of that?

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: (L.) Don’t know. Hawthorne, queer fellow, had relatives. Don’t see why he should ignore their claims. Do you know his history?

sp.speaker: Everard.
sp.p: Partially. I know that he was originally poor, but of a respectable family, and ——

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: (L.) In fact you know nothing at all about it; as it’s short, don’t mind telling you. I think his family never heard the word respectability in their lives, and I’m quite sure never understood what it meant. Hardly knew their own surname. George had some difficulty in ascertaining that, in after years. They were miners, or labourers in a mine—a coal mine; lived in darkness, and died in it; plugged up, or stifled off by choke damp, or some other infernal gas: that is, all of them except George, his scamp of a brother, and a cousin. George went to sea, enlarged his ideas, returned, became a workman in an iron foundry, got into the engineer’s room, showed talent for designs, learnt to read and write, and draw—saved money, made foreman, married employer’s daughter: employer died, wife died, became rich, invented all sorts of self-adjusting machines, built steam engines, laid railroads, built iron ships, speculated, made nearly a million of money, bought an estate; and, as he was a man of progress, and his manners in a great degree reflected the favourable change in his fortunes, found, when he was about fifty, an honourable lady—a poor baronet’s daughter—willing to marry him, and share his wealth.

sp.speaker: Everard.
sp.p: (R.) What an extraordinary career, to be sure.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p:
Not at all extraordinary—plenty of instances—national and individual—of progressive ascents from insignificance to wealth and name. Well, his second wife had no children, and he grew fond of her niece, the present expectant heiress, Miss Lucy Vavasour; but before adopting, or rather
becoming attached to her, finding that he was likely to be childless, he made some inquiries about his own relations.

sp.speaker: Everard.
sp.p: The brother and cousin you spoke of as having escaped choke damp?

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Yes; the brother he traced to a small maufacturing town in Lancashire, but learnt that he was a bad mechanic, a supposed good writer of songs popular among workmen, and a fluent tap-room orator when half drunk, which was usually the case four days out of the seven. Intensely disgusted with each characteristic, he determined to withhold all recognition of such a relative; but shortly before his death he again made some enquiries, and the fact of his brother’s demise, and of his one son’s industry, having raised him into a little independence, which enabled him to support his mother in comfort and respectability, and to look forward to a still better day, was modestly communicated to him by that son. To his surprise he learnt that his brother and family had all along been aware of his success; but from a dislike to subject him to a possible humiliation, had refrained from seeking his assistance in the hours of their greatest privation.

sp.speaker: Everard.
sp.p: That was very noble.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Perhaps it was—perhaps it was a stupid stiff-necked spirit of pride—don’t know which—but he was touched by it, and again first nature asserted her sway, and suggested the injustice of leaving all his property to a mere collateral relation, while kindred in whose veins his paternal blood flowed, lived, and were deserving, too; and still he felt bound by the moral obligation he had contracted to Lucy. The expedient Hawthorne hit upon we shall know to-day, when the will is read.

sp.speaker: Everard.
sp.p: Do you surmise its nature?

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: (L.) No; and if I did, I shouldn’t tell you one moment before the seal was broken upon the will.

sp.speaker: Everard.
sp.p: Quite right. What of the other relative?

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Oh, the cousin; he had married some petty farmer’s daughter in Lancashire, and had thriven well in a small way; he, however, made his exit from this world some time before Hawthorne’s demise.

div.stage: (Honorable Augustus Adolphus and Sir Lionel Norman laugh outside)

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Who are those?

sp.speaker: Everard.
sp.p: Sir Lionel Norman and the Honorable Augustus Adolphus.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: I object to both. The former is too fine a gentleman for my taste, and the latter too complete a puppy; let us walk and shun them for the present.

div.stage: Exeunt through window, L. C.
Enter Sir Lionel and Adolphus, L.; Mrs. Vavasour and Miss EUPHEMIA, R.; preceded by JEMIMA, who places chairs, and then exits, R.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Good morning, ladies. My dear Mrs. Vavasour, how do you find yourself to-day?

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: (seated, R.) As I always do in the country, Sir Lionel, alive, and little more: the air is too bracing for my delicate organization. Augustus, do me the favour to close that window. I am like an exotic, and can only live out of Belgravia in a conservatory, or a house of like temperature.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (aside, L.) Dear delicate old soul, how well she is preserved.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (aside, L.) It is the privilege of a lady to talk nonsense and the duty of a gentleman to sympathise.

div.stage: (Adolphus goes up and closes window, C.)

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Let me draw this screen between you and the possible draught from yonder door; and where is Miss Vavasour, has she not dawned this morning?

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: I really don’t know. Euphemia, where is Lucy, have you seen her?

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: I have heard—not seen. At present, I believe, she is in the gardens, or the conservatory.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: (R. C.) By herself, out of doors at this hour?

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: (R.) She had made an early breakfast and toilet, and knocked at my door an hour ago, wishing me to accompany her to some favourite points of view, but I was not ready, so she went alone.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: What robust habits the girl has, to be sure; acquired them from living as a child so much with Mr. Hawthorne.

div.stage: Lucy Vavasour appears on terrace, C.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
Mrs. V.
sp.p: Can that be Lucy’s voice?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Yes; here is Miss Vavasour.

div.stage: (Lucy opens window, runs in C., and closes it quickly, shutting out a little spaniel)

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (C.) No, no; you must not come in; go away—go away. Ah, Sir Lionel! Mr. Adolphus, good morning. My dear mamma—toilet made, and down, and looking so much better. Ah, how I rejoice! I knew the change would do you good. I vow you have a colour.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: (R. C.) I trust not—you alarm me—it must be hectic. You know the country air never agrees with me. Nothing but the necessity of attending the reading of this will could have induced me to risk the illness sure to follow.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Oh, don’t say so! I am sure that you must derive good from the purer air of the country. I feel as if in another and a much more delightful sphere. The emerald earth and azure sky, the fragrant, and yet nimble, jocund atmosphere, formed, as it were, of beams and ether, dancing from the highmost hill to mingle with the breath of flowers, murmurs of rills, and songs of birds, and all the other healthful, joyous pulses that beat mirthful music in the ear of dear old mother Nature—old, yet ever young—give to me a sort of second-better being.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Poetry!

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: Enthusiasm!

sp.speaker: Miss V.
sp.p: Nonsense!

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (aside, L.) Rubbish!

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (L. C.) I sympathise with your admiration of the Woodlands; it is a truly charming retreat.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: I expected as much from your good taste, Sir Lionel.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: The shrine is as it should be, in keeping with the divinity.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Meaning me. Oh, that compliment is extravagant and premature! I am not mistress of it yet—may never be.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: I wish, Lucy, you would not indulge in such surmises; they are very disagreeable, and cannot possibly be correct. Mr. Hawthorne could not have been guilty of such injustice.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: I shall never accuse him of injustice; he was always good and kind to me; but he may have had relations with stronger claims than mine: we should make some allowance for the interests of other people.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (L.) That depends upon the sort of people. Who are the others to whom Miss Vavasour refers?

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: (R. C.) I did hear from Dr. Playfair, that there were some nobodies summoned to hear the will; but they can only be the recipients of some small legacies.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (C.) Your nobodies must be somebodies, my dear mamma. Three or four of the best rooms in the east wing were prepared for them by Dr. Playfair’s orders; and I believe they have occupied them since last night.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (L.) Heyday! this looks serious.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: Low people in the house! what profanation! Please to ring, Augustus, for James. I must enquire who those people are—what they are like.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Nay, nay, dear mamma! Will not that be a little unbecoming?

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: At another time, under other circumstances, it might, but the interests of my child are at stake. There may be some conspiracy at work. I should get all information. Don’t you think so, Sir Lionel?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Well, it is but to inquire what company have arrived, and in that sense it cannot be considered a breach of etiquette.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (aside) Money is at stake, and etiquette may go to the devil both for fine lady and punctilious gentlemen. (rings) Oh, by all means, let us have James’s evidence, its sure to be amusing.

div.stage: Enter James, L. 2 E., crosses to L. C.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Ave I the ‘onour to be required?

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: Oh, James, be good enough to inform me who, and what sort of people those strangers are, at present domiciled here.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: {H}am I to report as, I think, madam—or {h}as I {h}am bound out of politeness to speak of any guests received into this noble mansion?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Ha! ha! a nice distinction.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Yes, yes, a capital opening. I foresee some strange revelations; let us hear his real opinions.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: The truth, James, as you think.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: By all means make a clean breast of it, James.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Then in my {h}opinion, madam, they are a rum lot.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: What?

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: I beg parding, but I always speaks graphically, and in keeping with my subject.

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Ha! ha! ha!

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (C.) Figuratively, I suppose, he means.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Yes, sir; I means figuratively and graphically too. They are, madam, a {h}invasion from the lower regions.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Figuratively also; no sulphur or brimstone odour, I trust?

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: (L. C.) Oh, no; but a deal of the clod-hopper odour about them. The fust that arrived was a young man, good looking enough, and not badly dressed, but one could see with ‘alf a {h}eye that he was no gentleman.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (R. C.) Indeed! why so, James?

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Why? why because he seemed rather out of conceit with himself, which no gentleman {h}ever {h}is.

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Ha! ha! ha! Capital.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: What a discriminating rogue!

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Ha! ha!

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: What was his name?

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Well, I think I knows, but I aint sure; there is some mystery over that part of my {h}information.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: The interest increases.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: Indeed! pray explain.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: He {h}arrived in a sort of a gig, or a dog cart, which was driven by a man in a rough dress.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: His servant!

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: I doubt that, for when he got down he shook hands with him, and said: “Well, good bye Hedmondson,” and the other replied: “Good bye, Frank, lad, and I wish you luck. Luck!”

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: Luck! Vulgar wretch.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Werry low, indeed, madam.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (L.) But what about the mystery—where’s the mystery?

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Why, sir, when we addressed him as Mr. Frank, he hopened his eyes, and looked hastonished like, and seemed ha going to speak, and then stopped short, and shut up as if afraid to speak, and afterwards muttered something to himself habout its being no matter, and discovered soon. Werry mysterious conduct, I call that.

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Ha! ha! ha!

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: A capital story; quite a goblin legatee.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: Now for the others, and don’t deal so much in detail.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Oh, the others are a greater mystery than the first; there are three of them.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: All in one; a triple ghost.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Oh, no; nothing ghost-like about them; very substantial, especially the {h}elder matron; {h}arrived in a flashy carriage and four, postillion and coachee, but only jobbed from the town where the railway station is; the company, fust a woman——

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Why not say a lady, James?

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Well, in compliment to the fair sex, I’ll call her a lady; rather a fine figure, but {h}excuse me {h}inclining, to or upon the {h}edge of being reckoned among the ‘as beens.

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Ha! ha!

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Daughter a nice looking girl, very tastefully dressed, and {h}exceedingly genteel {h}in manner compared to the {h}other; {h}and a son in the most vulgar, flashy style of getting hup that was ever seen at a country fair, or an ‘alf-bred race-course. They made noise enough, and {h}examined {h}everything. The mother said the room into which they were shown was fitted up with gimcracks and tinsel thingumbobs, not worth much.

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Ha! ha! ha!

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p:
The son said the sofa on which he stretched himself was first-rate—a right
down plummy one. {H}excuse this 
 language, it was theirs, not mine;
but the daughter said that everything was in exquisite taste.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: The young lady seems to be a favourite with you, James.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: A case of love at first sight, eh, James?

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Oh, no. I know my station and my dooty to society too well, and I left the room rather than loose my ‘art. Left alone, they wanted refreshment, and rang the bell. When {h}asked what they required, the old lady said, “Best in the house.” I stared, and she said I needn’t, for that she could pay for it. As it was late, I {h}enquired which they preferred, dinner or tea. “I’ll none of your slops of tea,” said the mother; “I’m right down faint wi’ thirst.” “So am I,” said the son; “but I want summat to eat.” I suggested wine and seltzer water for the lady. “Wine—rubbish! it always made her ill; she wanted a mug of beer.” I was so horrified, and in such a state of confusion, that I didn’t know whether I was standing on my ‘ead or my ‘eels.

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Ha! ha!

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Poor James. I quite sympathise with you.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: The young lady, stepping forward, said: “Be kind enough to bring, or order, some refreshments, such as are usually set for a light supper or for luncheon, with a little ale for my brother and mamma; and, please, a cup of tea or coffee for me.”

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Good little fairy, that was prettily said.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Well, I thought so myself, miss, {h}at the time; {h}at least, it was a speechies of refined {H}english that I could understand.

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Ha! ha! ha!

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Oh! James, James! you’ll be the death of me.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: Well, now for the names of these anomalies.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Oh, that the {h}old lady told me and the other servants twenty times—Mrs. Betsy Wildbriar, her son Robin, and daughter Jenny Wildbriar.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Is it possible—our Lancashire witches memoried by months of laughter? (crosses to L. C.) Sir Lionel, don’t you recollect the ball at Preston, on the occasion of the militia being reviewed by old Sir Charles, where we saw those wild roses blossoming in all their native simplicity of blush and fragrance?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: I remember something of the occasion, but nothing of the people.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Oh, they are presented to the life. James, you are a capital word-painter—quite a character photographist.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: (L.) As I said before, sir, I ‘ave eyes in my ‘ead, and wasn’t born yesterday.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: (R. C.) That will do, James; you may retire for the present.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Very well, madam. I think I have opened their {h}eyes, and sustained the dignity of a {hupper} servant.

div.stage: Exit, L. 2 E.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: You really know these people?

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (L.) Met them at the militia ball last summer; they had just inherited—wonderful creatures—perfect studies for an antiquarian—original as Lancashire itself—descended in an unmixed line from the aboriginal British, or the first Saxon squatters on the banks of the Ribble—that is, the mother and son. Sir Lionel, try and remember them?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (L. C.) Well, I begin to recall something in connexion with them. Didn’t Everard fall in love with the daughter, and get laughed at by us all?

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Everard in love! That’s excellent!

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: To be sure; the girl as James reports, very nice and as ladylike. But the great joke was my telling the mother, in confidence, that Everard was a marquis—the Marquis of Banterdown—ha! ha! travelling incognito for reasons of state, and cautioning her not to address him by his title, or to betray the slightest knowledge to him of the secret,

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (C.) What a shame! I do not see the object of the ruse.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Oh, it was productive of the greatest fun in the world. She first told her daughter, then her son, then all her immediate acquaintances. Then her open admiration of Everard! She addressed him as Mr. Marquis, and then begged his pardon twenty times. Everard was surprised, but never knew at what we laughed, and, for mirth’s sake, don’t tell him now. Let us keep up the jest here. We shall have such a meeting, as I believe both mother and daughter are in love with him. Ha! ha!

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: I shall be no party to a practical joke.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: You will not interfere one way or the other—such vulgar creatures are not worth a moment’s consideration for or against. (knock at door)

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: What’s that?

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: Some one knocked at the door.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: Strange—this is not usual with the servants.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Oh, it’s one of our Wildbriars, or the goblin legatee—let us have them. Come in.

div.stage: Frank opens door, L. 2 E.—about to enter, perceives Ladies; hesitates; Gentlemen look at him with eye-glasses.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (L.) I beg pardon. I—I am afraid that I intrude.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p:
Well, you certainly do intrude if you have no further
intelligence or business to communicate, but your suspicions upon that matter.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Sir!

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Eh!

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Yes—ah! It is very possible; but I thought— that is, I know that I have business, if this is the principal drawing-room, as I was left to surmise. Have I made a mistake? if so, I beg pardon.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (L. C.) Why did you not ask guidance and information from one of the servants, instead of troubling ladies and gentlemen to instruct and chaperon you?

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Sir!

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Eh!

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (aside) Confound his impudence. Sir, I did ask information from a servant in the hall, at whose want of politeness in not answering, I no longer wonder, having met a greater rudeness in reply from a gentleman in the drawing room. As I know that I have business, and do not come here without authority, it is only in compliment to the ladies present that I retire for the moment, to spare them the annoyance of altercation.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Your chivalry is most distingué.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Much more than yours, Mr. Adolphus, in my opinion. (crosses L.) Stay, sir.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (C.) And in mine, too. Courtesy should never be cast aside by the gentleman. (goes back to table, R.)

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Mr. Frank, I presume?

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (surprised, L.) Eh! madam. Yes, Frank is my name—that is, one of my names.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (aside) Oh, most hybrids have two. (goes to Ladies, R., and then takes chair R. corner)

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: One of the relatives of the late Mr. Hawthorne, summoned to attend the reading of the will?

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Yes.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: In that character, let me, as for the time being nominal mistress of this mansion, assure you, that your right to enter this room is equal to that of any one here, and may prove greater. It is the hour appointed for the reading; the trustees and executors have arrived, I believe, and will be here in a few moments. Pray remain. I entreat: you will pain me by refusal. (he bows) Be seated, I beg. On that table you will find the London papers of this morning, I believe.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: I thank you. Much obliged; sorry to have disturbed you.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p:
Pray don’t mention it. These gentlemen did not, in the freedom and privacy of an almost family conversation, recollect that strangers had equal business here. I trust under
these circumstances, you will forgive my friend’s apparent want of courtesy.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Certainly, madam. I never indulge in anger after an explanation. Forget and forgive is a good—Ah! well, I— I am talking. I beg pardon; pray don’t let me keep you from your friends. (she curtsies, and returns to her chair, R.) Courteous and kind, as beautiful. The mistress? she deserves to be. (Frank sits at L. table, and takes up newspaper)

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: That was admirably done; ladylike in the extreme.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: You did quite right, Lucy. Adolphus was too severe; and, as lady of the mansion, it is your place to show courtesy to all under its roof; but you should not have raised hopes in the young man’s mind by talking of his possible inheritance.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Why not? I was only Mr. Hawthorne’s favorite, not his relative.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: What relative is the newspaper solitaire?

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: I can’t imagine. I never heard of Mr. Hawthorne’s having any relatives, although he too frequently alluded to his humble origin, and was fond of saying that he had no family connections, and was a self-raised man.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Surely, he did not spring into existence from the mouth of a furnace, to engineer his cradle, and model his own pap-spoon?

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Ha! ha! ha!

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: For shame. I will not listen to a jest in connection with his name. He was a great man of his talent, and a good one of his kind heart, and he always loved me.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Who could help that? (L. C.)

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: He was a rich man, and made himself so, and therefore devilish clever, I must own.

div.stage: Mrs. Wildbriar and Jenny appear in conservatory R. 3 E. Dr. Playfair at window, L.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Eh! but it’s a bonny, a beautiful place, and summat like Crystal Palace. Eh! there’s an old man will tell us the way. Here, you sir, which is road to thingumbob—what do you call ‘um room?

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: I object to that method of making an inquiry. I shall not answer any question put in such a barbaric dialect.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: What’s the man mean? I speak good English, don’t I?

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: I object to that assumption. I call it a most inexplicable jargon.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Eh! he’s either soft, or right down impudent.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p:
No, no; pray, mamma, give me leave. (crosses to L.) You will excuse us, sir, we are strangers here; will you be kind
enough to inform me where we shall find, or perhaps, as doubtless you are going there, you will point out the room in which the will is to be read?

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Don’t object to that; nice little girl. This is the room, my dear; permit me— (offers chairs at back, and advances) Good morning, good morning—(Ladies and Gentlemen rise) Miss Vavasour, ladies, gentlemen—so all the parties assembled and I saw from the terrace, Mr. Cunningham coming up the avenue; we shall read the will at once.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Eh! but my lad, Robin, mun be here first; do you know where he is? I mean Robin, my son, Robin Wildbriar.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Don’t know—and I don’t care. (aside)

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Drat the lad, I’m sure he’s up to some mischief. (comes a little forward and perceives others)

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Shouldn’t at all wonder, if he’s like his mother.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Eh! what a lot of fine folks, and nobody to introduce us. Jenny, lass, now’s time to show your education; just astonish them by doing it first rate. You won’t—then I’ll do it mysen.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: No, no, mamma; by and bye; it’s not the custom— this is a business meeting—no need of introduction—no acquaintance at present likely to be made; pray be silent and sit down.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Does lass think I’m ashamed of my name or mysen? I’m none going to sit mumchance when there’s people to talk to.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: But they don’t know you.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Well, then, I’ll make ‘em know me. Yon old woman seems to be mother of the family. I’ll speak to her first. (advances, C.) Dame, how are you? hope you’re quite well, as I am, thank heaven. You dunnut know me, nor I you. I’m Mrs. Betsy Wildbriar, fra’ Lancashire, and this is my daughter, Jenny—and a nice lass ou is too, and well edicated; better than her mother; but then her mother is none the worse for that;—we’ve comed up here to hear my lad and lass’s second cousin’s—my old man’s that wur, first cousin’s will read: not that we care over much for that, for, thanks to my poor dead and gone brother, who died of yellow fever in Ingey, we’ve got plenty of money of our own, and summat to spare for poor folk. And now I’ve telled you who and what we are, some one of you do the same by me, and put us all right and straight and comfortable like, that’s the way we always do. There, I think I’ve done that very well. (crosses, L.)

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Isn’t she a glorious creature? (crosses, C.) Allow me to have the honour of officiating, as I claim to be an old acquaintance of this lady, having had the pleasure of meeting her at Preston on the occasion of the pleasantest evening, thanks to her society, that I ever passed at a militia ball.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Eh!—what the Honorable Mr. Augustus Adolphus, Esquire!—aye, for sure, and how are you, lad? Eh I I’m so glad to see you, man—and here’s Jenny,—Jenny, come and shake hands wi’ an old friend: (Jenny advances, L., and curtsies) and how’s the Marquis? Eh! I forgot—how’s Mr. Everard Digby?—see how Jenny blushes—eh! she’s rather sweet there, I can tell you. Oh, don’t need to frown or deny it, lass; you’re as good as he—he’s but a man after all, if he is a Mar—. Eh! bother that cognito of his. I wur nigh letting rat out o’ bag.

div.stage: Enter EVERARD, through window—comes down, R.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: He’s here—and will be delighted to renew his acquaintance. Your charming Preston acquaintances, Mr. Everard!

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Eh! it’s him, sure enough; and how are you, my lor—Mr. Mar— no, I mean Mr. Everard? you’re looking prime. Here’s Jenny—same as ever; shy and backward as ever hur wur. Come forward, lass, and speak wi’ an old acquaintance.

sp.speaker: Everard.
sp.p: Ah, Mrs. Wildbriar (crosses to Jenny, and takes her up to seat, L.), and my piquante little partner in the Schottische; I am delighted to see you. Pray be seated—permit me— (leads Jenny to chair, and talks to her, L., at back)

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: He’s just same as ever—pleasant and social like. Eh! he’s a gradely man. I wish he wasn’t a Mar—bother that, I’m always boggling over it. If he were only a cotton spinner, or a farmer, he might have her to-morrow, and one hundred thousand pounds.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Permit me. Mrs. and Miss Vavasour; Miss Euphemia Cholmondeley; Sir Lionel Norman.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: What! the Baronite!—Proud to see you, sir (he bows formally) Isn’t he well, or has he the rheumatiz in his side, or pains in his back, that he bows so stiff and grand loike?

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: It’s his knightly dignity.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Oh, I hate such stuck-ups; the Mar—you know who—is twice as agreeable.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (R. C.) Dr. Playfair, who—

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: (coming down, L.) Objects to any character-painting, at your hands, sir.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: And this gentleman—

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (rising) Will introduce himself. I am, Francis, more commonly called Frank Hawthorne.

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Hawthorne!

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Hawthorne? Any relation to my old man’s—that wur first cousin—that wur?

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: His nephew.

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Nephew?

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Eh! then we are relations, too. You are poor Bill Hawthorne, the poet’s son, that used to write in Bolton Luminary, eh! I’m glad to see you, lad, for your fayther’s sake; he used to come often to our house when on his tramp rambles; many a meal’s meat he’s had wi’ us, and many a good bright shilling I’ve lent him; but he drank it all. Don’t be ashamed; he was a good-hearted man for all that. And your our Jenny’s second cousin? Jenny, come and shake hands wi’ your cousin, lags.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (going up to Jenny) Pray don’t rise; permit me; I have much pleasure in making your acquaintance. (shakes hands and returns to his seat)

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
Jenny.
sp.p: (comes down, C. L.) Hush, hush, mamma! consider there are others here.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Oh, I’ll speak my mind, if there were twenty times as many—that I will, for sure. (goes up stage)

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: A nephew! I never dreamt of the existence of so near a kin; but the moment I saw that man, I had an instinct of something wrong. He’s sure to have been left a large legacy.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: I hope so—in mere justice he should.

div.stage: Enter James, L. 2 E., preceding MR. CUNNINGHAM; all rise to receive him.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Mr. Cunningham, solicitor, trustee, and hex-e-cutor. (goes up, R.)

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: Good day—good day, ladies and gentlemen.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: (goes to him, and shakes hands; then returns to his seat) Ah, Cunningham! I thought that you were going to be late for once.

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: (looking at watch) Late—late; no—no; minute hand within fifty-five seconds of twelve o’clock—will to be read at twelve—never waste time by coming too soon or too late to an appointment. Are all the parties summoned here?

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: I believe so.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: No—no; my son, Robin, isn’t. Drat that boy, wherever can he be? Robin! Robin! (calling at window, C.)

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: I object to that noise—not here, will must be read without him.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Stop—stop a bit; here he is at bottom o’ steps. Here, Robin—Robin—this way, lad; they’re going to read will here.

div.stage: Enter Robin, up steps of terrace, vulgarly, but flashily dressed; trowsers turned up, and boots mudded.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Robin, lad, just come in pudding time—as you always do, when pudding’s first. Lots o’ fine folk here, and some old acquaintances. Take off your hat, make a bow, and I’ll introduce you.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: (R. C.) Na; I dunnot want no introduction. I can speak for mysen. (advances, C.) Lads and lasses, old men and old women, my service to you. I’m Robin Wildbriar; oh, I aint ashamed of my fease, or my name, for I’ve done nothing as yet to make me, and I don’t mean to; and if you can only—all of you—say the same, you may hold up your heads any whur. (Jenny signs to him) Eh! all right, lass; and now I’ve said my say, I’ll take a seat. (takes chair and sits, C.)

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: (R. C.) Good lad, couldn’t have spoken better if he had studied it out a book. Eh! where have you been your boots are all mud?

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Why, you see, I picked up an acquaintance with a terrier pup that belongs to these parts, and he and I have been rat hunting in the stream down there. I wiped boots dry upon the grass of the plat yonder; but if there’s any offence I’ll take them off, and put them outside. (Jenny comes down on his L., and signs to forbear) All right, lass. (rises) Mother doesn’t know Jenny’s dodge and mine—I’ll tell her. Mother, stick by me, and do as I do. Jenny and I agreed, as I wasn’t up to the ways of the nobs, for her to give me a sign whenever I put my foot in my mouth, and as she’s just done it, I’d better shut up. (all take seats—Robin puts his chair back)

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Silence for Mr. Cunningham,

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: (seated R.) One moment. James, you can leave the room.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: I beg parding, but as I was five years in the service of the lamented George Hawthorne, Esq., I though it just possible that I might ave a {h}interest, a slight one, of course, in the will.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Didn’t he pay your wages, man?

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: That’s right lad—an impudent lacquey.

div.stage: (Jenny signals again)

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Hold hard, mother; Jenny’s tipped the wink.

div.stage: Mr. C. Mrs. W. Everard. James. (table) Robin. (table) Sir L. Jenny. Mrs. V. Miss E. (table) (table) Augustus. Lucy. Frank. Dr. P.

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: James, you are not particularised in the will, I know; as I drew it up.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: That’s enough. (aside as he crosses to L.) You drew it up; you drawed it out, you mean; but I thought as much, as soon as I saw his low connexions {h}arrive. {H}ingratitude {h}always {h}accompanies {h}ignorance.

div.stage: Exit, L. 2 E.

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: As the will will be hereafter open to every one’s perusal, I do not think it necessary to read the somewhat lengthy preamble, which sets forth the testator’s history, and some motives which induced the disposition made by him of his property.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Aye, don’t give us any preamble.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Come to the point, lad.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Hush, hush.

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: I shall only read the marginal abstract of its several bequests, and thus dispensing with the lengthy forms and technicalities, put you in immediate possession of the whole purport.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Speak plain English, man, and let us ha’ no climclavers.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Shut up, mother.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: I shan’t; and you had better not insult me before people, or I’ll give thee a clout on the side of the yead.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Silence, woman!

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: (coming down, C.) Woman!—who do you call woman? I’m as good as you, and have as much money—may be more; I dunnot care none for your will. I can do about it, and if you speak two words more, I’ll go out o’ the house, now then.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: I say, you’ve napped it, ould chap; haw, haw! (to Dr. Playfair—Lucy rises and goes to Mrs. Wildbriar)

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Pray excuse the doctor, madam; he is one of the executors, and is only anxious that the will may be read without interruption, and I am sure, as a good lady, you will excuse him, and oblige us all by listening in silence. (goes back to seat)

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: You’re right, lass. I’ll do anything for fair words, but I wunnot be driven.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Na, I’m sure ou wunnot; mother’s like a pig feyther had—(Jenny checks him) All right, lass—mum.

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: (reads) “I, George Hawthorne, formerly of Alnwick, Northumberland, and now of the Woodlands, Dorsetshire, being of sound mind, declare this to be my last will and testament, and do hereby give, will, and bequeath all and several of the various sums to the parties herein named and described in this my will. Imprimis.—To my second cousin, Robin Wildbriar”

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: That’s me.

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: “And his sister Jane, or Jenny Wildbriar—”

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: My lad and lass first.

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: “The sum of two thousand pounds each.”

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Well, we could ha’ done without that.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: It isn’t much, but it’s a summat, lad, as a token of remembrance; we’re independent, thanks to my brother. (Lucy casts a glance at MRS. WILDBRIAR) All right, lass; (to Lucy) I’ll say no more.

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: “Items—To my old and valued friends, John Playfair, and William Cunningham, whom I appoint joint trustees and executors of my will, the sums of two thousand pounds each.”

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: That’s as much as he has given us, his cousins.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: May be they want it, poor creatures, they look shabby enough.

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: “Item—To Mrs. Sophonisba Vavasour, relict of the late Henry Vavasour, the sum of two thousand pounds.”

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: That makes ten thousand pounds in all as yet.

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: “Item—To my nephew, Francis Hawthorne—”

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Eh, that be my cousin—which be he? (Mrs. Wildbriar points to Frank, Robin rises and goes to him) I mun shake hands wi’ him; how is thee, lad?—I’m glad to see thee; I am for sure. (shakes hands with Frank)

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Silence, and sit down.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: I mun speak to my cousin.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Sit down; sit down do—or leave the room.

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: Sit down; sit down, sir.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Get off wi’ you! (sits down sulkily)

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: “Item—To my nephew, Francis Hawthorne, a life interest, chargeable upon the general estate, of three hundred pounds per annum.”

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: I breathe again.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: So do I. The heiress is now clear.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: And is that all to his own nephew?—shame of hissen.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Hush—hush, mamma.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: I cannot—I wunnot; it’s a shame, and I’ll give him summat mysen.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: So will I; we munnot see cousin wronged. Cheer up, lad.

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: “Item—To my second wife’s niece and long adopted daughter, Lucy Vavasour, a life interest, chargeable upon the general estate, of five hundred pounds per annum.”

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Why he’s given her more than his nephew.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: Great heaven, five hundred pounds a year, is that all? My poor child! And what becomes of the rest?

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: I guess; an hospital for ricketty engineers.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Or a bluecoat school for miners’ illegitimates.

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p:
“And all the remainder of my property and
personal estate, inclusive of the mansion and manor of the Woodlands, the several farms and rent-charges attached, and held under the same freehold, together with various sums of money vested in railway scrip, government securities, Three per Cent. Consols, Exchequer Bonds, and Bank Stock, &c., &c.,—”

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: (aside to Everard) Nearly a million.

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: “I give, will, and bequeath—ahem!”

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: To—to whom?

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: “Jointly—to the said Francis Hawthorne and Lucy Vavasour, on condition of their entering into and fulfilling a contract of marriage within six months from the date of the publication of this my will.”

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Eh! that’s capital!

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: First-rate!

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Singular!

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: Wonderful!

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: Horrible!

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Damnable!

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: ‘And in the event of the said Francis Hawthorne refusing to comply with the prescribed condition, I give, will, and bequeath the whole of the above-named property to the said Lucy Vavasour, her lawful heirs and executors.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: He’ll be none such a fou—eh, lad?

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: “Also, in the event of the said Lucy Vavasour declining to contract the conditional marriage, I give, will, and bequeath the whole of the said property to the said Francis Hawthorne, his lawful heirs and executors.”

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: She’ll never be so soft—eh, lass?

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: “And in the double event of the said Francis Hawthorne and Lucy Vavasour mutually declining to accept of the condition, I give, will, and bequeath the whole of my property and estate, subject only to the payment of the aforesaid legacies and annuities, in trust, to my executors, to build and endow a foundation school and hospital, for the pauper orphans of miners, born in the neighbourhood of Alnwick, Northumberland, subject to the annexed rule and conditions.” which I presume, it is quite useless for me to read to the present company. And now, there are six months, short of one hour, left for the interested parties to determine their acceptance or rejection of the condition; during which, this mansion, with its usual establishment of servants, &c, will be at their service, for the entertainment of their friends and families, in accordance with the testator’s directions to the executor’s, endorsed upon this his will.

div.stage: (all rise and put back their chairs except Lucy and Frank, who sit on opposite sides of stage, L.C. and R. C.)

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: My poor child!—disposed of!—doomed!

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: No! neither disposed of, nor doomed. I am still mistress of myself; and who knows—it may be for the best?

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: I wish you joy!

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Joy—joy!

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Joy—joy! who knows—it may be misery?

div.head: ACT II.
div.stage: A lapse of three months is supposed to have occurred.

div.head: Scene same as in Act I.
div.stage: Enter James and Jemima, R.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Yes, Miss Jemima Simperton, {h}of all the {h}extraordinary positions that a {h}upper servant {h}as ever been placed in, mine is the most trying and {h}inimical to my feelings, {h}ever since that {h}eccentric will of the formerly late lamented, but now {h}ungretted George Hawthorne, Esq., was promulgated, I ave been in perpetual ‘ot water. How can a {h}upper servant preserve his dignity to the lowers, when he don’t know who is his master, and when he has to deliver his {h}orders as from one to the {h}adherents of the other?

sp.speaker: Jemima.
sp.p: It is very difficult indeed; and nobody but yourself, Mr. James, could manage to preserve any appearance of regularity in a house under such divided control. As in the play, we have the Capulets and Montagues on each side, never meeting but to wrangle.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p:
hexcuse me, but the play aint a comparison at all—hit’s {h}only {h}image is the House of Commons, with two {h}oppositions, a {h}independent country party, and some {h}outsiders, that don’t know their own minds; and I’m in the position of the unfortunate Speaker, who is obliged to listen to all the jaw (eloquence, I should have said, of the {h}uppers—the lords), and for the sake of the respectability of the ‘ouse itself, is compelled to keep up a {h}imposing {h}aspect of attention, and a wigilant {h}eye on the uproarious junior, and Hirish members; and I see no prospect of a coalition neither; the R. L. Adolphus. Robin. Jenny. Everard. Miss E. Sir L. Mrs. V. Lucy. Mr. C. Mrs. W. Frank. Dr. P.
leaders—as the leaders always is—are too polite to look each other in the face.

sp.speaker: Jemima.
sp.p: (R.) Miss Lucy will never marry Mr. Hawthorne, of that I am sure: she was always partial to the baronet, and you see he is still here and attentive.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: (L.) So he is; but he is only a trimming, I can see, and {h}aint popped the question yet, and never will, if she loses the fortune: he’s poor, and his ‘igh blood is worth a fortune, and he’ll not sacrifice at the ‘ymenial {h}alter unless Plutus gives away the bride.

sp.speaker: Jemima.
sp.p: What a fine figure of speech to be sure. I should have thought that he was above mercenary motives.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: So he is; {h}it’s his dignity that requires to be upheld by the needful, and he must victimize his affections to the {h}integrity of the baronetcy, which is mortgaged to the very verge of bankruptcy.

sp.speaker: Jemima.
sp.p: If Mr. Hawthorne were only a gentleman now, he might be induced to sacrifice the fortune and decline the condition.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: If he did he would prove himself a demned fool.

sp.speaker: Jemima.
sp.p: Mr. James!

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: {H}excuse the wigorous expression; but even an {h}aristocrat of the purest Norman blood could not be expected to resign his claim to such an inheritance on account of being inimical to the co-heiress. The days of such high-flown sentiments and factions is faded and gone. We sacrifice our {h}affections, but we never sacrifice our worldly interests. It {h}aint the fashion in {h}upper, or even in middle life. Such things are only heard of now in {H}east {h}end melo-dramas.

sp.speaker: Jemima.
sp.p: What a nice little bit of flirtation there seems to be going on between Mr. Everard Digby and Miss Jenny Wildbriar.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: I ‘ave observed that, and I hardly think it fair. She is the only member of her family that’s deserving of respect, and he ought not to try and turn the girl’s head if he don’t mean marriage, which her low birth and connections would make disreputable for a barrister so highly connected.

sp.speaker: Jemima.
sp.p: I don’t know that; he seems very much smitten, and he’s kept in countenance by the Honorable Augustus Adolphus, who has begun to ogle that vulgar old woman, the mother, and Miss Euphemia Cholmondeley, who actually affects a sentimental attachment towards the cub of a son.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p:
You ‘orrify and astonish me with the noos. What is the {h}aristocracy coming to after that? Pounds, shillings, and pence is a hobject—‘as ever been, I know, with the best of us—but some regard for position, for {h}elegance, bought to accompany it. Miss Jemima, if such should be the case—I—I
shall leave the {h}aristocracy to hits fate, and whilst I can preserve some recollection of its ancient dignity, retire into private life, and not {h}even {h}open an ‘otel nor a liquor waults. As my favorite {h}author says—

lg.l: But give us still our old nobility.”

div.stage: Frank Hawthorne enters from library, L. 3 E. He looks round, then comes down and gazes a few moments on the picture of Lucy, R.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Like; very like—and oh, how beautiful! Not the beauty that dazzles at the first glance—dulls upon the second— to grow vapid, vulgar, at the third—the rarer beauty of expression, whose fascination steals over you as you gaze, and seems to wile away your soul. And yet, I think a high spirit lightens in those eyes. She will not sell herself for gold, she will sooner accept poverty. How to win her confidence, to read her heart, to conquer it? No way. She must hate me as the imposed and conditional husband, linked to no generosity, the representative of the niggard doubt that tasked the golden boon. Seldom as we meet, and quickly as we turn from each other, as by some repellant instinct, I have noted that her cheek grows pale, and despite of her self-control, an expression of repugnance—a shudder passes over her form. Well, well, the time will soon come to spare her that; and yet would I give the world to prolong the ordeal that sometimes yields her to my eyes. Yes, yes, to win her smile, I think I could play slave and fool unto the very verge of baseness.

div.stage: Enter Dr. Playfair, L.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Umph! in the absence of the original, I see you make love to the portrait.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: I—I? oh, no. I am an admirer of good pictures, and I was just noticing the exquisite finish of this portrait. I presume, from the style, painted by—by Landseer.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p:
I object to that. If you had looked at the whole picture instead of the face only, you would have remarked that there was no dog in it, and therefore not likely to be by Landseer, who in my opinion in addition to being one of the best painters, is the greatest satirist of the age, for he never paints man or woman unaccompanied by a puppy. (Frank laughs) There, I’ve got you nicely out of your anger, and your shame too. I object to both; particularly to the latter. No reason to be ashamed of trying to scrape acquaintance with your future wife’s character through the medium of her portrait. As yet
you haven’t had much chance of studying the original: you ought—time’s getting on.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (R.) This gentleman may mean well, but he lays too rough a hand upon the wound to be entrusted with its cure. Sir, if you speak in badinage, you are quite welcome to a laugh at my expense; if seriously, allow me to say, not in anger but earnestly, that this is one of the subjects upon which I neither give confidence nor take advice. As for the picture, if you will look at the scroll work in the left corner, you will find Edwin Landseer’s pencilled autograph, who, in this instance as I dare say in many others, having to paint a truly beautiful woman, thought but of his subject, and left—the puppy out.

div.stage: Exit, R. 3 E.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Out—outside I suppose the fellow means, implying me. I’m afraid its a truth. I have no business to bark at his heels, since he objects to it. Confound him! he always gets the better of me at the wind-up of a dispute, and hang me if I don’t begin to feel a friendship for him. I shan’t be able to object to anything he says by and bye.

div.stage: Enter Lucy, Mrs. Vavasour, and Sir Lionel, R. 1 E.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (L. C.) Oh, here is Dr. Playfair, and he will give us his advice.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: I object to that. Never give advice—it’s a complete waste of time and thought; for although everybody asks and endeavours to extort it, no one ever allows it to exercise the slightest influence upon purpose or opinion.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: That may be the rule, Doctor, and you will allow me to be the exception.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: I object to that. Why should you be a greater fool than your neighbour, as you must be if you permitted anybody’s judgment but your own to guide your conduct?

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Well, but, Doctor, you and I have always been friends, and surely I may task your kindness to the extent of an opinion upon a matter that deeply affects my welfare.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: (L.) Well, I don’t object to that; you are one of the very few people I like, so now propound your problem, and I’ll solve it if I can.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Well, the question is, what am I to do with him?

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Him—and who is Mr. Him? Your lap-dog, with the snuffles in his nose, or your cast-off beau, with the jaundice in the whites of his eyes?

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Oh, for shame! I am serious, and you know very well the disagreeable him of whom I speak.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: I object to that, because I don’t.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: It’s Mr. Hawthorne. We want to devise some means of preserving the property, and disposing of him.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: The readiest way to dispose of him, is to marry him.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: You wouldn’t have my daughter marry a clod?

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Why not?— It was a clod of the same clay that made all the money; but I object to the term clod, it’s not apposite. If you will be figurative, call them nuggets.

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Ha! ha! ha!

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Ah, well, the particular nugget that remains I don’t like.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Well, do as I tell my squeamish patients with a draught or a pill, gulp it, and take the sugar after.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Oh, to me, this is rude and unkind.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: So it is, and I beg pardon. If you really wish to keep the property——

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: Oh, that doesn’t admit of a question. (seats herself at R. table)

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: And to get rid of Hawthorne?

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: That I certainly do.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: You must offend him, and incur his dislike.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Surely I have done that by avoiding him.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: I object to that—worst way in the world; he was rather struck with you, and sees only the romance of the tie formed by the will. He’s in love with you: I caught him a few moments since gazing in silent devotion on that picture of yours;—he’s always to be found on the terrace, or at one of the windows, slily peering at you every time you go out into the grounds; in fact, he watches and worships your rising and setting, as a fire-worshipper the sun, or a lunatic the moon.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: (R. C.) Vulgar creature!

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (at table, R.) Impudent puppy!

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (L. C.) Poor fellow!

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: (aside) She pities him; and I don’t object to that. He dreams of you in your retirement; and his fancy makes you twice as handsome as you are.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (curtseying) Thank you, doctor.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (comes down, R.) Surely——

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Don’t speak, Sir Lionel. I know you were going to say that that is scarcely possible.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Bosh!

div.stage: (Sir Lionel goes to table, R.)

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Ha, ha! you hear! but don’t interfere—the doctor and I are old friends.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: And gives you four times the number of virtues and graces that any woman possibly can possess. In short, he idealizes you into a divinity; and all this is due to your avoidance.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p:
Now, doctor, you are really too ungallant. Surely you do not mean to imply that my presence must necessarily
dissipate any good opinion that may have been previously formed of me?

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: That depends upon your conduct. He’s sensitive and high-minded, and seems to have a good stock of common sense; you have only to surprise him with a little drawingroom coquetry; alarm him with an exhibition of frivolity; vex him with some heartless badinage; and season the whole with a huge admixture of twaddle (you must know how, having lived so long in good society); and, my life on it, he’ll reject you.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (L. C.) Ha! ha! ha! the doctor’s recipe for getting rid of a sensible, sensitive, high-minded man.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: (aside) About whom you begin to feel an interest, despite of your professed dislike. It will bring them together; and if she likes him, she will soon give over playing a part.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Well, I’ll do it; that is, I’ll try to conceal my natural graces—ahem, doctor—and to assume as many disagreeable airs and follies as the aforesaid will permit.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: But what if the fellow should prove, as I think, a mere simpleton, and not take umbrage at this treatment?

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: As a dernier resort, I can always decline.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: (rises) And lose the property, my child! you must not entertain such an idea.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (aside) No, no; for then to decline would be my dernier resort.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: He’s coming this way to have another gaze at the picture. Present him the original, and commence your game.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Well, if it must be done, the sooner it is got over the better.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Shall we remain?

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Oh no! you must be my reserve corps, upon whose aid I can fall back, if necessary; at first, I must attack the enemy single-handed.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: You’ll find him a formidable foe, or I am much mistaken; he has always put me to the rout.

div.stage: Exeunt Sir Lionel and Mrs. Vavasour, R.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Dear me! Now that I am alone, I begin to feel a little nervous, and my heart flutters. I wish I had not heard of his admiration of my portrait, or the doctor’s encomiums on his good sense. It is not pleasant to be obliged to incur any sensible person’s contempt. I feel a great disposition to run away. No, no—I am too English for that. He must not see me when he enters, or perhaps he’ll run away. I’ll entrench myself in this chair, and not open fire until the enemy is within range. (she sits in large easy chair, L. C.)

div.stage: Enter Frank, R. 3 E. from conservatory, looks round, and comes down as before, R.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: It is true—my portrait, sure enough, is the object of his attention.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Heigho!

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: He sighs, poor fellow—fool, I should say; and yet I pity him. Heigho!

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (overhearing, turns and perceives her) Miss Vavasour!

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: La! Mr. Hawthorne, is it you? (smiles graciously)

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Yes; I beg your pardon; I didn’t know that you were here—I shall retire.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Oh, pray do not! Is my company so very—very disagreeable, that you can’t endure it for a moment or two?

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Oh, no—no. Can it be possible you think that such is my feeling?

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: I must think so, if you continue to run away every time we chance to meet.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: True—true; but I was only fearful of intruding upon you an unwelcome presence.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Oh, that doesn’t matter; it is—heigho!—our duty to become acquainted. It is not, Mr.—Mr. Hawthorne?

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Yes; that is, if it is your pleasure.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Oh, I dare say I shall have much pleasure, if you’ll only permit me. Pray, be seated. (he looks awkwardly around) Do sit down.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (taking a chair at a distance, R.) There is something under this sudden change.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: I can’t look at him. Excuse me, will you do me the favour to hand me my fan? You will find it on the table near you. (he rises and gives it, and returns to his chair) Thank you; I am sorry to trouble you, but I’m so affected with ennui this morning, that I can’t bear to get out of my chair, and in a tête-à-tête with a gentleman, I can never speak, or even look at him, save from behind my fan. (aside) He’s rather good-looking, but awkward to a degree.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: My idol’s feet, I begin to fear, are made of clay. Or is this a ruse? If so, I’ll balk it. She thinks me fool. I’ll play the fool.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: I saw you looking at my portrait. Do you think it like?

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: I did—very like.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Did, but don’t. I’m getting on famously. Didn’t you think it rather dreamy-looking with that sort of expression the critics term ideal, but which I call moonishness?

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (seated R.) I think it conveys the expression of which you speak.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p:
Which does he mean, I wonder? Oh! then it must
be very unlike me, for I’m not in the least moonish or poetical. Will you pardon me? I have some crochet work in that little basket on the table over there, which you will favour me by handing. (he rises and brings it from upper table, R., takes fan, and places it on table) I can work now, as I have got rid of the nervousness consequent upon our first tête-à-tête. You needn’t wander all the way back to that distant chair, there is one close by.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (aside) This is barefaced coquetry, or it masks some designs. Thank you—as you please. (takes chair R. C., and seats himself)

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: As I please! How gawkily done. You admire the Woodlands?

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Much. Eh!—that is, I think it a nice place enough.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Nice place! and that is all he has to say of my beautiful Woodlands. He’s either soulless, or vexed; so much the better. Will you excuse me again? I think I should prefer my embroidery to this crotchet work, Mr. Hawthorne, you’ll find a frame and design on that table to my left hand. I can’t shake off this ennui to-day, and am as changeful in desire as a child. I’ve all my life been a spoilt one.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (gets embroidery frame from L. table, and comes down L.—aside) So it would appear; and yet there is a soul-like light flashing from under those half-closed eyes— there is a melody of tenderness rippling through those affected tones, that, like spells, charm me into yielding to this folly.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (seated, L. C., pouting) No silk on the reel!—oh! here is a skein. Ahem! Mr. Hawthorne, will you think it too feminine a task to hold it on your hands whilst I wind? Hercules, or some very heroic gentleman of olden time, I believe, did something like it.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (L.—aside) I don’t wonder at him, if Omphale was anything like you.

div.stage: (he holds his hands awkwardly and close together, she puts them apart—he then raises them very high and afterwards drops very low)

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: You are too tall, or you hold your hands too high— and now too low. See, there is a footstool upon which you can seat yourself. (pushing footstool towards him—he sits on low stool, L.) Docile and patient as a spaniel. (winds silk, and labours to restrain laughter) Ha! ha! ha! Excuse me, Mr. Hawthorne, but—but you do look so ridiculous. Ha! ha! ha!

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (on the stool) I dare say that I do. Ha! ha! ha!

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Oh! you can be good-tempered enough to laugh at yourself.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Perhaps I should not, if I had not perceived almost from the first that you were acting a part, and disguising your real nature for some purpose.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Pray pardon me, sir, I confess it. (rises, crosses, R. C.)

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: You do? Ah! that is honest, and I most sincerely admire your frankness, and forgive the ruse. (rises)

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Oh, dear, what have I done? I’m spoiling all. Yes—yes, I had a motive—a purpose—one I didn’t mean to tell you, though; but I suppose I must, now. I—I wanted to see—to see how you would go in harness.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (aside) Harness! what does she mean?

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Yes, you know that under our circumstances it wouldn’t be comfortable to find ourselves straining to go separate roads when we are harnessed.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Excuse me, but I don’t understand what you mean by being harnessed.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Why, married, to be sure. The will says we are to be; and what is marriage, but the fettering of two parties together whose tastes generally differ, but whose interest and convenience it is to travel the same road, and not quarrel by the way.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Dare I tell you what I think marriage to be?

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Oh! yes, I should like to know your ideas. I have told you mine.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Pardon me, I think not. You have but echoed the cant of the blazé inconstants—the reckless roués of the world—who seek to bury principle in the grave of passion. Marriage is at once the most solemn and the most beautiful mystery of this life; and as among nations, a reverence for its sacredness, as an indissoluble, an untransgressible bond between two beings, and only two, is the highest test of civilization, so the nobler the estimate individual man or woman forms of its obligations, the higher must each be lifted in the scale of humanity. Do you not believe me?

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: I do, I do, with all my heart and soul.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Ah! I thought so.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Oh! I’m ensnared again! I can’t play the game any longer without aid. (laugh outside, R.) Ah! here comes that Mr. Adolphus, and my beau ideal of a preux chevalier, Sir Lionel Norman.

div.stage: Enter Mrs. Vavasour. Augustus, and Sir Lionel, R. 1 E.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Ha! ha! a capital design! I’m so glad that you have made me a party to the joke.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: (R. C.) Yes, it’s a disagreeable necessity (affecting surprise) What, Lucy and Mr. Hawthorne tête-à-tête?

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Yes, mamma: you know it is necessary that we should become acquainted.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: So, the play begins again.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Oh, yes! Let people be ever so unlikely to be found together after marriage, it is customary to preface the ceremony with a little show of sociality.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: Oh, of course; we do as we like afterwards. He can speak tolerable English, I suppose. (seats herself)

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Oh, yes, mamma; he speaks very well, and is not near so dull as you might imagine. (seated)

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Is this said to test the extent of my love, or to offend me?

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: He bears that; his spirit has sunk many fathoms deep in love or stupidity.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: As he has just come up, as it were, from the other world, no doubt you found the gentleman’s conversation highly entertaining?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Yes; of its very novelty, however bizarre, it must have served to amuse.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Oh, dear, no! it was edifying. Mr. Hawthorne is rather of a serious bent of mind; and on the solemn subject of marriage, did not fail to improve the occasion.

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Ha! ha! ha!

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: What, a Spurgeonite! imbued with the afflatus of the conventicle!—the large, the very large conventicle! Catch-em-alive, oh! He would make thee a sister of grace before leading thee to the tabernacle. (goes up L.)

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Ha! ha! ha! (Frank laughs heartily)

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: The fool laughs at himself.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Really, that pathetic mingling of piety and passion— of the parson in the Romeo—the puritan in the Corydon— seems to me as the most incongruous—the most laughable of bipeds.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: I have never beheld one when the spirit moved: I should so like to hear him hold forth.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (crosses to Frank) Perhaps the gentleman will oblige us?

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: He doesn’t understand, and is silent.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: I addressed you, sir.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Did you? Then allow me to say, that I could not have imagined so fine a gentlemen capable of such point blank rudeness.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Oh! I vow he’s angry! (goes to Frank) Now, my dear fellow, be good humoured. You really mustn’t attempt to sermonize us. (turns up stage)

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p:
No; If you can’t lightly give and take, nor yet endure the privileged sarcasms—the badinage of drawing-room
society—you had better not venture within its pale. (goes up, R., and sits)

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (crosses to Frank) Sir Lionel is quite right; you must not lose your temper at a little banter: if you can’t reply in the same spirit, you had better hold your tongue. (crosses, R.) Sir Lionel, I go to make my toilet for our drive. Mr. Hawthorne, we shall meet again soon, I hope. Why, what’s the matter? (returns, L.) You are not jealous of my going to take a drive with Sir Lionel?—you must not, because I like Sir Lionel, and I hate jealousy. I shall expect you to prove a very gentlemanly husband, and to forgive all my caprices and follies, and extravagancies—and I’m over burdened with them, I can assure you; if you doubt it, ask my friends yonder, and they’ll give me such a character: even Sir Lionel will tell you that I am very captious; but then, you know, I have always had my own way, and we are to have so much money, that were I even wicked, you could not help but have me. How you look! Oh, no! I don’t mean quite that; I am not so unwomanly.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: I thought not.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: There, I’ve forgot again; (crosses, R.) so be good humoured when next we meet, and we may be good friends in—in time. If that doesn’t determine him to reject me, my game is played out.

div.stage: Exeunt Mrs. Vavasour and Lucy, R. 1 E.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: We may be good friends in time—never, never— did you mean what you say? but you don’t—you don’t; your lips scorn the words that cross them, and your eyes recant them quicker than your lips pronounce, or love conspires with thee to make me slave and fool.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: The fellow is a complete puzzle; he’s not likely to be shaken off.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: I’m afraid not, and your chance of adding the Woodlands to the entail of the baronetcy is but slight, but you will have a beautiful wife.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (R.) Perhaps; but she must be an heiress.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (L. C.) Oh, lady heiresses are few and far between. Our friend, the Marquis of Banterdown, has wisely enough marked down the nabob’s niece.
sp.stage: Frank re-enters, to take his hat and gloves from table—they do not perceive him)

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: She is a very nice little girl. (seated) How she ever grew upon the Wildbriar tree is a puzzle; but the mother and son are insufferable.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Oh, the Wildbriars are not so bad after all. Country bred, with amiable aspirations.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Ah, you have been taken into confidence, Adolphus—and see them by the light of a lac of rupees.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Ah, well, their manners are certainly rustic—I may say very rustic—but time will amend these—must, for the widow has been left £250,000.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: You astonish me! £250,000? Why, that in amount treads upon the heels of the Hawthorne fortune.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Yes, she talks of giving son and daughter each a hundred thousand, and of keeping fifty for herself, to make presents to her granchildren, when such buds blossom.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Well, although whilst a hope remains of her inheriting, I’m the Endymion proper, most professedly a minion of our Diana of the Woodlands, I think I could contentedly retire from the chase, if that little Hebe, Miss Wildbriar, were to be caught and hid away from her Satyr-like kindred.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Ahem! (they start and turn) Good morning, gentlemen.

div.stage: Exit, L. 3 E.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (rises) Confound it, that fellow here? I hope he has not overheard my last remark.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (rises) If he has, what does it matter; he can’t translate it. Your classical metaphor were as much a riddle to him, as Greek proved to the majority of a reformed House of Commons.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Ha! ha! true, it is likely; but Miss Vavasour’s toilet is, I dare say, complete.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: And Diana awaits her Endymion.

div.stage: Exeunt Sir Lionel, R., Adolphus, L.

div.head: Scene Second.
div.stage: A Chamber (1st grooves).
Enter Mrs. Wildbriar, R.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
