sp.p: Here, Jenny, Jenny, lass; Hob, Rob, where are you? Drat that lad and lass, they’re always out of the way when I want them.

div.stage: Enter Adolphus, L. 1 E.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Did I not hear Mrs. Wildbriar’s voice? Great heaven! whom do I behold; surely my ear must have deceived me, and yet the contour, the figure can only belong to one— it is Mrs. Wildbriar.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Yes; I’m mysen sure enough. What is’t makes man doubt?

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: This magnificent toilet has so added to your natural advantages, that, joined, they took me by surprise; and I fancied I beheld a still more beautiful woman.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p:
Got away wi’ you. I’m well enough, but I’m not
so soft as to swallow all that blarney; I don’t like saft sawder, so ha’ done wi’ it.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: I beg your pardon, but I assure you that that dress becomes you amazingly.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Oh, the dress: well, I think it does favor itsen— it ought, for it cost enough of money. Eh! I dunnot mind telling you it took a start out of me when it was first brought home. My servant lass had put it out in the middle of my bedroom, and it was standing up of itsen, and spread out, so that when I saw it, I thought some one was inside, and going to run away wi’ it, and I shrieked out for help—eh! didn’t my lad and lass laugh at me—it was the iron thingamygigs inside—they call ‘em crinoline—that stuck it out like a balloon, and I wouldn’t wear none o’them: I couldn’t ha’ gotten out of door if I had put it on; I’m sure I couldn’t, I’m big enough ‘about.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Oh! you are by no means remarkably embonpoint.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: That’s French for plump, isn’t it? I’ve heard it before from my Jenny. Eh! I was as slim as she is when I married my poor old man; he wouldn’t know me now were he alive.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: He must have been a very happy man. Heigho!

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: What’s the matter wi’ the man, aint you well, that you breathe so hard?—hast the cholic-spasm, or a stitch in thy side? You can cure either immediately by taking a sup of burnt brandy.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Oh, no; it’s an affection of the heart.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Heartburn, mayhap?

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Yes, my heart does burn.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Well, a little soda, or a bit of magnesia, will make that all right; if they’re not handy, nibble a bit of chalk. I often do myself.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (aside) Ugh! sentiment here is thrown away; where ignorance is bliss, ’tis folly to be wise. No, no, my dear madam, it was a flutter of the heart, to which I am always subject—(aside) in the neighbourhood of any woman worth a certain number of thousands.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Flutter of the heart, eh? I never had that but once, when I saw my old man in company with another lass, just a day before he got my leave to put up the askings; and he owned to me after that, that he did it on purpose to make me not hold him too cheap. Ah! he was a sly chap, my mester, but a good man for all that. But tell me, have you seen my lad or lass anywhere?

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: I think Miss Wildbriar is walking in the avenue with Mr. Everard Digby.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p:
With the marquis? I’m afraid he’ll turn the girl’s head; if he doesn’t mean to marry her it’s a great shame, and
so I’ll quickly tell him. She’s as good as he, if he were twice a marquis, and may be has more money; for I’ve heard that some of these lords and ladies are just as poor as they are proud. (Adolphus looks abashed, thinking the conversation levelled at him) Eh! and do you know I begin to think that that stuck-up Miss Euphemia is as sweet upon my Rob as the marquis is on Jenny. I wonder where she is?

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Ahem! I think I may gain favour by revealing the truth as respects Miss Euphemia. May I propose a stroll while I impart a secret to you, Mrs. Wildbriar? (crosses to R.)

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: I wanted either the lad or the lass to keep me company, as I have got the rheumatiz in my ancle, and must have some one to lean on; and besides, I hate walking alone.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: I shall be most happy to be permitted to offer my arm; pray lean on it.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Eh! it aint much of an arm you have to lean on, man; I should tire you.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Fear not; animated by a spirit of admiration, it would sustain you for a life time.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Eh! it ‘ud break in two under half my weight in a jiffey; but as I cannot do ‘bout a stick of some sort, I may as well have you as another, and I shan’t lean heavy on thee; so come along, and tell us your secret.

div.stage: Exeunt, R.
Enter Robin and Miss Euphemia, L.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: I say, mother!—oh, she’s gone.

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: And so, Mr. Wildbriar, you don’t share in the general admiration bestowed upon the Woodlands?

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: (R.) Oh, the place itsen is first rate; but there aint no pleasant sociable company in it—all stuck-up baronets and honourables, and fine ladies with their noses in the air. There’s that cousin of mine, I did think to have found a bit of a chum in him, but he’s no better than the rest: don’t care a bit about a rat hunt, ferreting rabbits, or bowls, or skittles, or dog fights. Jenny is in love with one of the stuck-ups, and mother thinks o’ nothing now but dressing and undressing hersen; and if it weren’t for that terrier pup, I should have no society at all out or in doors.

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: (L.) Oh! don’t say that; I’m sure I’m always happy to meet you, and ever find pleasure in your conversation.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: No, do you? well that is kind of you; but may be it’s because no one else cares much about speaking to you;— excuse me, I come fra Lancashire, and always speaks my mind. (aside) except when sister Jenny’s by to put the stopper on.

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: I admire your candour; and it may be a sympathy arising from the neglect we mutually experience, that suggests the friendly interest that exists between us.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Oh, I have no interest at all in the matter; you speak to me, and I speak to you in return, and much obliged. I’m a civil lad, and no wastrail. Are you fond o’ toffey?

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: Toffey!—I must plead ignorance of what it is.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: No! Where were you brought up?

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: In Paris principally.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Ah, that accounts for your not knowing English. It’s made of sugar and butter baked in a pan. I’ve got some real Everton—brought a stock with me—and I’ll let you have a lump when I see you again.

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: I shall prize any gift of yours.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Eh! you mun eat it, woman, and not prize it. I say, you don’t like their manner of living in this house, do you?

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: Ahem!—not much.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Neither do I;—there’s no getting a meal in decent time; they eat their breakfast when they should be taking their dinner, their dinner when they should have done their tea, and their tea when it’s supper time, and their supper when they should be waking out of their first sleep.

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: Earlier hours would certainly be more healthful.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Aye, that they would; and what’s worst of all is the lots of slops and made-up dishes that come on the table before you can get anything substantial to eat, and nothing much then. I did think to enjoy my dinner yesterday, because there was a giblet pie, but there was no black pudding in it, and it warn’t worth much. You like black puddings, of course?

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: Black currant puddings?

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: What!—Black currant pudding in a giblet pie? Noa, black puddings made wi’ pig’s meat and groats or oatmeal, Eh! when we lived on the farm we always had such a stock; mother makes them prime! but what’s worst here is that they never have a dumpling to begin with. You know what they are, I suppose?

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: Yes, when a child I remember being partial to an apple dumpling.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Oh, I mean suet dumplings; hard ones, to eat wi’ gravy, pepper and salt, or it may be treacle—good hard ‘uns; them’s the chaps to fill up; if I could only catch mother out of a fine dress, I’d persuade her to go down into the kitchen and make some, just for once; she makes them better than any one else—first rate. Oh, you should taste mother’s dumplings.

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: I should be most happy.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p:
Would you?—then, ecod, I’ll get mother to make some to-day, eh? This is the only bit of pleasant conversation I’ve had since I’ve been here. She’s a stunning nice girl after all; rather thin, but dumplings would bring her round soon. She seems to be sweet on me, and I’m in a great mind to make
up to her. Hem!—that is the way them stuck-up chaps do— Hem! hem!—Were you ever in Lancashire?

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: No; I should like to visit it much—it’s a delightful expectation with me.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: No; is it? I say, you’re a rare nice wench to talk to. If I were to offer to gi’ thee a buss, you wouldn’t lug or pow my hair, would you?

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: I really don’t know what you mean.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Don’t understand Lancashire English at all?

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: I must confess ignorance of those words.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Ah, I shall be obliged to give her up; never get along wi’ her so ignorant.

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: But I should be very willing to learn.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Noa, would you?—ecod, then I’ll begin to teach you. You see, this is a buss. (kisses her, she slightly screams) Enter Sir Lionel and Everard, R., she runs off, L. Eh, don't be frightened, lass; I don't care the toss up of an old button for these chaps, wi' all their grandeur.

sp.speaker: Sir L. | Everard
sp.p: Ha, ha, ha!

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Haw, haw! wwhat are you laughing at ? I'll be bound that both on you ha' kissed your sweethearts in secret as well as me, and when you're alone — you wi' my sister, and you wi' Miss Lucy, I don't interfere or intrude on you — do I?

sp.speaker: Sir L. | Everard
sp.p: Ha, ha, ha!

div.stage: Cross stage and exeunt, L.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Haw, haw! — you may haw, haw, as much as you like; but I'll fight either of you, with or without clogs on, Lancashire fasion, on the grass plat yonder if you please.

div.stage: Enter Mrs. Wildbriar, R.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: I’ll fight you, if I hear you making such a noise in the house. What’s up wi’ you at all, man?

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Well, them two stuck-up chaps were laughing at and jeering me, because they caught me give Miss What-you-call-her a buss.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Eh! did you give her a buss?

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: I did.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Buss her again, lad, if thou likes it; I didn’t think thee had so much pluck in thee. Let them laugh as wins. Gie me a buss, lad; you are as good as they wi’ all their parade, and I’ll make thee some dumplings for thy dinner.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Noa, will you? Hurrah! Dumplings for ever!

div.stage: Exeunt, L.

div.head: Scene Third.
div.stage: The Central Space within a maze of shrubberies. A little fountain margined with flowers; seats set around; the termini of the paths opening on stage, and describing a crescent, the front of stage being supposed to complete the circle; some of the shrubs in blossom, others with beds of flowers underneath; small terminal statues, with parasites twined round pedestals, &c., at back above the shrubberies a side view of the house; lawn; woods beyond.
Enter Miss Jenny Wildbriar, R. 4 E.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Mr. Everard Digby does not seem to know his own mind, except to the extent of being in love with me—that’s of course. Very innocent or very stupid he must be, if he cannot see that I am only waiting to be asked: to blush, hesitate, look aside, and say, or rather sigh, Yes.—This problem I ought to solve, will solve, shall solve. Oh! I know I shall, for I know that I’m a clever little girl, and that he’s in love with me, and that I’m in love with him, and with plenty of money there can be but one solution—marriage! Bravo! clever little girl. But it’s still to be wrought out:—how? Make him jealous. Yes: of whom?—the elegant but sly Sir Lionel Norman, who has seen that I am pretty and well-mannered, if not so handsome or high-bred as Miss Vavasour, and possibly may prove richer, and so spares me a little indirect attention, just sufficiently pointed, when I meet him alone, to suggest a motive, and to be an excuse for a grand avowal, if that should hereafter prove politic. Clever man; but what of that? there never was a male coquette a match at his own game for a clever little girl. Oh, he is here.

div.stage: Enter Sir Lionel, R. 1 E.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: One hundred thousand pounds, with a reversion of twenty-five thousand pounds at the least; and she’s pretty, very pretty, and surprisingly well-mannered. Soft! she is here. Young girls like sentiment, and adore heroics. Ahem! “Is it a nymph, a naaid, or a Grace, that with her presence queens this lovely place?”

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: (L.) Sir Lionel, is it you? Oh, dear! Strange too: although you have taken me by surprise, you have caught the very spirit of my day-dream, echoed the very motive of my musing.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: And “What didst thou muse on, meditating maid?” Shall I go on? “On earth thou standest, thy thoughts ascend to heaven.”Quoted from John Home's Douglas (1756)

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Oh, dear, no! mine were very terrestrial thoughts, I was just thinking what a beautiful place the Woodlands is, and wishing ——

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: To be mistress of such another.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Nay; it is too large a setting for a little Lancashire diamond, too extensive a kingdom to be queened with becoming dignity by such a little rustic body as myself. I should like to steal a nook out of it, like this; no grand woods, but orchards and shrubberies, and a petite lawn, that a couple of limes or sycamores would serve to embower, and a low, quaint, old-fashioned house, cloisteral in its outward appearance, but luxuriously elegant, and yet cozy, if I may couple the terms, within. I should feel at home there; a sort of Rosamond in her bower, or Amy Robsart at Cumnor:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amy_Robsart_(play) the Woodlands is too much of a Windsor, or a Kenilworth, for poor little me.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: What charming naïvete! Of all wildbriar roses, surely this is the prettiest.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: I have heard it said that Norman Abbey is not unlike my ideal, Sir Lionel; is it so?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: You have poetized, and yet most faithfully described my ancestral home.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Oh! I should so like to see it.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Would you? you may—you shall, if it so please you.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: And yet, no; for if it should realize my dream, I should be loath to leave it.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (aside) I begin to think that I should be loath to let you leave.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: And you know that I should have to do that.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (R.) I don’t know any such thing; there could be no more welcome presence to the house or to the master.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Oh! now you flatter.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Indeed, I do not: short as our acquaintance has been, Miss Wildbriar, I assure you that it has sufficed to awaken feelings in my breast of the warmest admiration, and the most earnest respect for my new and charming friend. (aside) That is hinting a great deal, and not saying too much.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: And this is not a mere compliment?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: It is a profound truth.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Heigho!

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: You sigh; surely I have not offended?

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Oh, no. Have you to learn, Sir Lionel, that sighs are sometimes expressive of happiness?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (aside) That is a fair challenge, but I must not go too far. May I, in remembrance, and in token of the acknowledged sympathy between us, present you with this beautiful camelia japonica?

div.stage: Enter Everard and Frank at back, and come down, L.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Thank you. Sir Lionel. My cousin and Mr. Everard Digby.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (aside) Confound these fellows, they seem to be ubiquitous. Yes, it is a very beautiful specimen of the genus, indeed.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: (aside) Oh! that is to imply that I have been showing it him, and asking his opinion; but you shant get off so. Sir Baronet. Look, Mr. Digby, at this rare camelia japonica, which Sir Lionel has just presented to me; is it not beautiful?

sp.speaker: Everard.
sp.p: It may; I have not much taste in these matters, but it seems to me common enough. (goes up and seats himself, R.)

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: (aside) That is because you look at it through green spectacles. So much the better. What think you, Mr. Hawthorne, of Sir Lionel’s camelia?

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: It is very pretty, but Sir Lionel is mistaken, it is not a camelia, it is a new and elegant variety of the common pansy, vulgarly termed, “two faces under a hood;” (aside) and the most appropriate present, as the most emblematical of himself that he could make. (goes up and sits, L.)

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (R.) As I am not a gardener, professional or amateur, I may be excused the mistake. Miss Wildbriar, I salute you for the present.

div.stage: Exit, R. 4 E.

sp.speaker: Everard.
sp.p: (R.) She was flirting with him, and he with her. Oh, woman, woman! always caught by the tinkling sound of a title. I cannot hide my anger, and yet I cannot be rude to her. I shall go.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: (C.) Surely you are not going, Mr. Everard, soon as you have come?

sp.speaker: Everard.
sp.p: I came to look for Miss Vavasour, I was told that she was here, or at least in the shrubberies. Excuse me; I have some business to communicate.

div.stage: Exit, R. 3 E.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: (R.) He is jealous—jealous unto vexation, and is gone to meditate revenges—deep revenges! Delightful! he’ll soon come back to my feet—my very feet—and beg me to put the chain around his neck, and then for the solution, and a sly laugh at sly Sir Lionel. Bravo! clever little girl.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (rises, L.) Miss Wildbriar, or rather let me call you cousin, as I would be privileged to speak seriously.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: My dear cousin, I shall be most happy to listen, for I’m in excellent spirits, and almost need a sermon to keep me from laughing.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p:
(comes down, L.) Because you have just given pain to a sincere admirer, by flirting with an affected one. Cousin of mine, that is not the pulse of the honest, unsophisticated heart of girlhood, as it should beat in the breast of an English farmer’s daughter. It is the tricksy, inconstant setting of the artificial woman’s, met with but in Vanity Fair, and whose
nominal heart is just as substitutive for the one she has lost, as her false locks or her false teeth are for those she has shed.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: That’s very harsh.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: It is true. We are out of our place here, let us not lose here the virtues, the absence of which would make us out of our place at home.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Cousin, you are a very superior man, but you will allow that another member of the family may be clever too—not to the same extent, for you’re a great man, and I’m only a little girl, but on certain subjects clever little girls are just as wise as great men; and now let me give you a bit of advice, not in the way of retort, but out of a cousin’s regard and a woman’s respect for your good heart and your great talents, which she does not like to see subjected to the double grief of insult and disappointment.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: I guess—I guess; but go on—I listen.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: You are more out of your place here than I am. You love Miss Vavasour, and by the tenor of the will, despite your low birth, you are authorized to seek a return of your affection. That is hopeless. She was all but pledged previously to Sir Lionel Norman. She would have refused you at once, but was over persuaded by her mother and friends to wait the issue. She has been further persuaded to play a part likely to offend or provoke you (as your admiration has been apparent), in the hope that you would decline, and so dower her with the fortune. If she should be induced, as by Sir Lionel’s doubtful conduct and her mother’s persuasion she may, to accept you, it will be for the money, and not love, nor even liking. (Frank goes up L.) Forgive me, for I see this pains you deeply; but I believe that you would rather know the truth, for all the pain of the moment, than be the dupe for life.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (L.) You have read me rightly, and I thank you; yes, I thank you from my—my heart. My heart! no, no—not from my heart—my slavish, bruised, my coward craven heart, that cries out beneath the lash, and weeps upon its wound, but from my soul;—the man’s soul that rises up within to put its heel upon that heart, and crush it out if it should groan again. (crosses to R., then back to L.) I thank you, all is well. I understand the comedy. ’Tis well—’tis well; we’ll play it out, but with a change of parts. (sits, L.)

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: (R.) I am almost sorry now that I have cautioned him. He has bit his lip until the blood has started; but he has a great soul, and the strife fought out ’twill gain the mastery. That’s the way we English win our battles, little people as we are. Oh! here comes a goodly assemblage.

div.stage: Enter Sir Lionel Norman and Miss Vavasour, R., Hon. Augustus Adolphus and Mrs. Wildbriar, L., Everard Digby, Mrs. Vavasour, and Dr. Playfair, R.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Ah, here’s a bonny place, and seats too; that’s right; for I’m right down tired, and want to rest mysen. (sits down)

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Yes, this, of all places in the grounds, is my treasure trove—my sanctuary. Heyday, ’tis already occupied. Mr. Hawthorne and Miss Wildbriar tête-à-tête. Faun and wood-nymph. Oh! it is my turn to be jealous now.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Thou hast no need, lass—second cousins can’t marry—first may.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Oh, indeed—a consolatory regulation.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (L.) To be found only in a Lancashire rubric, I should say.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (R.) He doesn’t speak; my play begins to tell. I’m glad of it, for I tire; and I don’t want to earn his contempt, as well as his dislike.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: (R.) I should object if you did; (aside) but I’m afraid you have.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: (L.) This place must ha’ cost a deal o’ money.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Yes; it was arranged under my direction—all the shrubs and flowers are rare exotics—not more beautiful than some native to our gardens; but then the price gives them an additional value.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: (seated, R.) I object to that; in my mind, it takes away from the value.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: (seated, L.) That’s the only sensible word you’ve spoken to-day, old man. I always think more of a thing the cheaper I get it, when it’s a bargain.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: I object to that; I don’t believe that you are any such exception to the general rule of your sex.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: I don’t care what you think; I tell you it is so.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (seated, R.) Dr. Playfair—a word—you spoil my play; you must, for once, get off your hobby-horse, and leave all the objections to Mr. Hawthorne. (aside) Oh, for my part, I value most things by their price. I like extravagance, and think in dress, flowers, bijoutry, it is a woman’s privilege. Don’t you admit that, Mr. Hawthorne?

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: No.

div.stage: Sir L. Adol. Mrs. Vavasour. Mrs. Wildbriar. Lucy. Everard. Jenny. Dr. P. Frank. L.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (coming down, L. C.) Only no! I should like to have a reason for that no.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (L.) Indeed! Well, as it may add to your limited stock of ideas, I shall give you one.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Sir!

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (rises) Oh, you object to drawing-room badinage in the open air, do you?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (R.) Ha, ha!—the retort courteous. Adolphus, you must accept it good-humouredly.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Oh, yes; but nothing vexes me so much, as being accused of a paucity of wit.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (L.) As the conscious insolvent is always most indignant at the suspicion of poverty. (takes stage and back to situation)

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (seated, R.) Ha, ha, ha!

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Not bad—yield him the honours, Adolphus—the Dacian is only learning to fence, and handles his foil like a club. (they go up, R.)

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (overhearing, aside) Does he? You had best mind your fence, or it may tap at your ribs before long.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: We still lack enlightenment, Mr. Hawthorne: your reason against extravagance—woman’s pet privilege.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (C.) Extravagance is a waste, a vice, and a vice cannot be the privilege of either sex, much less of the weaker and more delicately organized. Over indulgence of any desire—intemperance—a riotous, inconsiderate waste of money in the gratification of any passion—the antetype of excellence, cannot be any part of refinement of taste. It is a debauchery.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (seated) Oh, really this language is too bad.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: It’s insufferable!—offensive in the extreme!

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (rises) You have indeed made use of a shocking word, Mr. Hawthorne;—it wounds the ear.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: I am glad of it; but the habit of extravagance is much worse—more vicious than the word—more hideous behind any mask it may assume; it vitiates and leaves a gangrene—a slime upon the soul.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Truth—truth! I can’t object to that.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: But I do; for it’s a very disagreeable doctrine. Our little elegancies of costume—our delicate devices of ornament for boudoir or bower—as they improve our appearance, and shed light and fragrance on our journey through life, lift us above the level of the mere sensual wayfarers, and repay the expenditure.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (looking at her) Sensuous; if not sensual. (retires to his seat, L.)

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: That arrow sped; the bear has retired to his den to grumble over his discomfiture.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p:
I shall make him come forth again, and speedily.
Do you go and talk to Sir Lionel. He’s looking the other way—(rising, and crossing to shrub opposite) How beautifully this magnolia blossoms. I must have a sprig—(plucks one, and then in returning to seat, deliberately throws handkerchief down) Mr. Hawthorne—Mr. Hawthorne, you are remiss.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (L.) Indeed, on what account?

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (R.) Sir, do you not see my handkerchief?

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Oh, you wish it to be picked up?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (who turns and hears) What a boor—permit me. (advances, C.)

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Oh, no; having asked Mr. Hawthorne, I can receive it only from him.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Oh, if the gentleman can acquire a merit in the performance of the office of a lacquey, by all means let him. It is a service I should never volunteer.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Indeed! may I ask why?

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Because I could not suppose you to be so foolish as to throw your handkerchief down, for the mere purpose of having it picked up again.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Throw it down?

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: You certainly did. I saw you.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (aside) So, he was watching me, for all his pretended indifference. I confess it. I was anxious to test the difference in the politeness of certain gentlemen, and I have succeeded. (goes to seat—Sir Lionel picks up handkerchief, and presents it to Lucy)

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: I am glad of it; it has, doubtless, enabled you to distinguish the man from the fopling.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Sir, you are personal.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (rises, L.) Mere drawing-room badinage; and you must make allowance for the Dacian, who is only learning to fence, and handles his foil like a club.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (R.C.) Ha! ha! the retort courteous; you must accept it good-humouredly, Sir Lionel.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Yes; but hang it—a fopling!

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: I admit your objection to be classed with the mere neophytes and tadpoles, such as your friend; and will acknowledge you to be a master in the craft—a B.A. of the foppish beau-monde. (goes up, L. C.)

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Ha! ha! ha!

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: I can’t object to anything this fellow says. I shall be obliged to avoid his company, or I shall lose the only pleasurable excitement that life affords me. (seated)

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (R.) He’s rather a clever fellow.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: He’s a d—d deal too clever; and I rather suspect that he has seen through our game from the first. (goes to ladies, R.)

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: If so, how he must despise me—with what a sense of superiority he must look down upon this petty device.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Bravo, cousin; he begins to understand the game, and to play it well.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: (without) Mother—where’s my mother?

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Here, Rob. Eh! now I shall have some one to speak English to me, and needn’t sit mumchance any more. I haven’t understood a word that has been spoken here for the last half hour. What does thee want, lad?

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Eh! all the nobs here—and Jenny, too—to frighten the thoughts out of my head, whenever I open my mouth, with her winks and nods? Mother, I mun speak in private wi’ you.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: No, lad; speak up—whispering ain’t good behaviour in dacent company. What’s got to say?

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: What’s o’clock? my watch has gone down; but I know by my stomach that it must be near mealtime, and them dumplings mun be done.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: (up L. C.) No, lad, no; they’re big and hard, and take a deal of boiling. Eh! the damp grass has brought my rheumatiz on again—help me up, lad.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (crosses to her) Allow me.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: No; I dunnot want thee now. I’ve got my lad, Robin. Get off wi’ ye. (Adolphus crosses back)

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: (comes down) Where’s thy lass, lad?

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: (R.C.) Eh! she’s gone home by hersen’—she wouldn’t come here—she’s sheamfaced, I suppose, by reason of these chaps laughing at her.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Corydon without his Phillis. Ha! ha!—Mrs. Vavasour, the gentle Euphemia has proved susceptible, and made a conquest, too, at last.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: What mean you?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (seated, R.) Behold the Adonis who has charmed that hitherto marble heart.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (R. C.) Adonis!

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: What’s he calling me names for?

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (coming down, R. C.) Cymon—Cymon, you mean—the veritable Cymon—“the clown who never dreamt of love,” and “whistled as he went for want of thought,”—chancing at last upon his destined Iphigenia here, is about to undergo the wondrous transformation which love alone achieves on clowns.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (C.) True; whilst the ductile nature of a gentleman, self-interest, sways at will, without apparent change, to jeer the clown, and woo his mother.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Woo the mother, eh?

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: What's that, Adolphus?

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (R.) Oh! nothing. Some more badinage, which of course I must take good-humouredly. (aside) Damn his quick perception! he is Argus-eyed. (goes up, R., to Ladies)

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (coming down, L. C.) Robin, lad! How ist thee?

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Eh! you can speak Lancashire English?

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Yes, lad, and I needn’t be ashamed, for it’s getting into great favor in this quarter. Which is to take place first, thy mother’s wedding, or thy own?

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: My mother’s? Haw! haw! haw! She’s none going to make such a fou of hersen.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: I don’t know that. There’s a gentleman here so much attached to you and yours, that he’s anxious to become thy stepfeyther.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (comes down, R.—aside to Frank) Sir! sir!

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (aside) Badinage! badinage! take it good-humouredly.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: A gentleman! No—eh! Shan’t I have such a laugh at mother. Which be he?

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Oh! Of course the gentleman who accompanies her in her morning walks, and who just now was so desirous to save you the trouble of helping her from her chair.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: What, that whipper-snapper? What, that chap with the Billy-goat beard? Haw! haw! haw! (Frank goes up) He, my feyther! Mother! mother! thee ain’t a going to make a fou of thysen, and get married again, art thee, and set all the neighbours laughing at thee!

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Noa, lad. What’s put that in thy head.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: (R.) Because that chap yonder is making-up to thee.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: It’s nought o’ sort!—it’s nought o’ sort!

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: He wants to be my feyther. Haw! haw! haw! What a feyther he’d be!

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Get along wi’ you. If I thought he wur, I’d box his ears.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: It’s for sure. What does he go a walking wi’ you for, and a sniggling up to thee, and offering his arm? It’s thy money he wants, and not thee, woman.

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Ha! ha! ha!

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Eh! if I was sure he meant it, I’d break his head.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: I shouldn’t object to that.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: How dare he think a woman of my time of life would have the loikes of him, a laced-up dandy, wi’ a waist as small as my Jenny’s? Eh! if I wanted a husband, which, Heaven be thanked, I dunnot, it’s summut of a man to look at I’d have, and not a hop-o’-my-thumb skipjack. He’d better not come near me agin, or I’ll slap his face.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: (C.) Do as you used to do to me, mother, lug and pow his hair, and it will be sure to come off, for I know it’s a wig. Haw! haw! haw!

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: (L.) I’ll cuff thee if thee laughs when I’m in a passion. Didst thee mean it, man—did’st thee mean it? (rushing up to Adolphus)

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: No, no; I assure you, on my honor, it’s all a mistake.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: I object to that; I believe he meant it, on my soul.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: (C.) A mistake! well, I hope it is. You mun excuse me, ladies, for getting in such a passion, and making so much noise, but the idea of his making up to me! He might as well to you, Mrs., and you’re older than me by long chalks.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: (R., seated) Oh, dear no! Was there ever such a vulgar woman?

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Oh! well, if it’s displeasing to you, never mind; but it’s plain to be seen, for all that. I always speaks my mind.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: That’s right, mother; so do I! no double fence about straight-forward Lancashire folk.

div.stage: Exeunt Mrs. Wildbriar and Robin, L. C.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (seated) Some merit in their candour.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (seated) It would be none the worse if graced by a little courtesy.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (comes down, C.) True; but courtesy ceases to be a grace, when used as a cloak to deception.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (rises, R. C.) The emphasis of your remark seems to level it at me, sir, but I am not pregnable to the point.

div.stage: (Lucy advances, R., Jenny, L.)

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Achilles upright in his armour, but mine is only club wit, and boasts no point at all; and yet, abroad in the Woodlands, I thought your Arcadian reveries might have suggested a supposititious Endymion, who, in the chase of a Diana, had marked down a Hebe among the satyrs, as a prize positive, if the superlative divinity should escape.

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Ha! ha! ha!

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Sir, this inference is impertinent.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: What, you bleed? But I forgot when Achilles knelt at Hymen’s shrine, he laid aside his armour, and proved vulnerable in heel as well as heart.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Sir, I—I—damnation. (crosses to L.)

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (C.) Why, I vow he’s angry; my dear fellow, if you can’t lightly give and take, or endure the privileged sarcasms—the badinage of drawing room society—you had really better not venture within its pale. (goes up, R.)

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (comes down, R.) Ha! ha! fairly foiled, Sir Lionel; don’t be angry. I am most undeservedly complimented by the comparison, and feel honoured in being accounted first prize by the refined taste, whose second may so fairly claim the soubriquet of Hebe. Miss Wildbriar—I mean my dear Hebe— will you stroll with me, and be Diana’s nymph for the nonce? Dr. Playfair, give your arm to mamma, and lead her to the house. (crosses to C.)

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: (aside—rises, R.) I should like to object to that, but courtesy forbids.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Gentlemen, adieu! don’t follow, or beware the fate of Actaeon. Mr. Hawthorne, we shall meet, I hope, at dinner.

div.stage: (curtsies—he bows—Lucy and Jenny exeunt at back, C.— Dr. Playfair and Mrs. Vavasour, L.)

sp.speaker: Everard.
sp.p: You have fairly avenged yourself.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (L. C.) No; I have but vindicated a man from the assumption of a clique.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (R. C.) He shall apologise, by heaven; he shall instantly, and before Digby.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (R.) Should he refuse?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (R. C.) Refuse—refuse; he shall not—he dare not, for the consequences. Mr. Hawthorne, you have been rude to insolence; had you been the clown you were supposed, I should have treated it with contempt. I pay you the compliment of admitting your intelligence and equality, by demanding an instant apology.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (L. C.) I do not appreciate the compliment. Had you been the gentleman you assume to be, you never would have committed the error of premising that a stranger, however diffident, and humbly born, must of necessity be a clown. I treated that insolence with silent contempt, until it became the assailant. I have made use of its own weapons to retort, and I shall certainly not apologise, because in the contest you began I happen to have so far succeeded as to have ruffled your equanimity.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: You have taken an unfair advantage in the repetition of words not intended for your ear.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: You meant to take an unfair advantage in assailing the barbarian, as he was deemed, with the weapons of your supposed superior civilization.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: To end this, sir, I demand an apology.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Which I decline to make.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: I thought so.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Then, sir, you compel me to waive all further courtesy of consideration, and to seek the satisfaction usual amongst gentlemen.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: A duel? I thought that had become unusual amongst gentlemen; but I forgot, you would rather type one of the actual paladins of the past age, than be the representative of their spirit in the present. How if I refuse?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: I shall feel authorised to speak of you in public as an insolent, and a coward.

div.stage: (Lucy and Jenny pass to and fro at back)

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: A disagreeable alternative enough, although a man of sense might hope to live it down. Let me see; I heard you boast the other day that you were a capital pistol shot.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: As you may be.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: I am not good enough to make it a boast.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (half aside) So much the better.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: The assassin-like sentiment of your friend, Sir Lionel, I will suppose you are too lineal a descendant of our chivalric conquerors to echo.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Confound his quick ear.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: You said, at the same time, that although you had been a pupil of Angelo’s, from your disinclination to enlarge or afflict with ridges your characteristically small hand, you had never practised with the broadsword; neither have I, and my hand, by some extraordinary freak of nature, is as small as yours. The only shadow of a grace that can belong to a duel, is that which makes the chances equal between the belligerents. Suppose we send for a couple of Andrew Ferraras,https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andrew_Ferrara and like doughty knights of old, cross blades, cry havoc, and lay on.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Sir, the proposal, if seriously meant, hath an air of complete savagery. It would sink us at once to the level of mere barbarians. No, sir, to that I cannot consent.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: But you would level your pistol at my body, in the confidence of a skill certain to send a bullet through heart or brain. The savagery is worse, because more cold-blooded and assassin-like. (crosses to L.) Pshaw! appoint your time and place,—bring your pistols. (crosses to C.) I shall prove as good a shot as you: I fear not the skill that shrinks from an encounter with an unexpected danger: ’twill lack both physical and moral self-possession in the trial.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Enough, sir. To-morrow, at sunrise, beyond—

div.stage: Re-enter Lucy and Jenny.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (C.) No, no; this must not be, for my sake. I entreat—I implore—I command! (to Sir Lionel) Were you to kill him, I should hate you. (to Frank) Were your hand to be stained with his blood, it—it would break my heart.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (L. C.) True, and in either case, you could hardly become his wife. Well, well, I can risk all chance of cowardice to play the true gentleman. I own to having taken a somewhat unfair liberty with words lightly uttered, no doubt, in jest, and not meant to reach my ear, and for that I beg to offer an apology to Sir Lionel.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Which he most readily accepts.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (sinking on her knees to Frank) Bless you! Bless you!

div.stage: Jenny. Adolphus. Sir Lionel. Lucy. Frank. Everard.

body.note: Another lapse of three months is supposed to have occurred.

div.head: ACT III.
div.stage: Scene.—Same as in Act I.
Frank discovered, R.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: The last day of doubt has passed; within an hour or two must hesitation cease, and yet she gives no sign. True, since the day on which I was surprised into a betrayal of my feelings, and then goaded into a retort of her friends’ scorn, her manner has changed. It is now deferential and observant, at times hesitating and nervous, as still doubtful of the result. Hopeless of inducing me to recede, and persuaded by her mother and her friends that it were better to sacrifice her feelings than the fortune, she accepts me as a fate, and is endeavouring before marriage to accommodate her manners unto mine; a submissive baseness of soul, of which I did not think she could have been guilty. I wish that she had kept up the mask—the play; it was finesse, but still warfare, and I could have carried into solitude a reverence for the idol of my dream, though that idol had not listened to my prayer.

div.stage: Enter Dr. Playfair, L.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Alone, and soliloquising? I object to that upon your wedding day.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: My wedding day?

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Yes;—if by twelve o’clock you are not married, the fortune either goes to the lady, or comes to you, or is locked up in trust for an indefinite number of unknown brats. I object to that, and to the principle of Chartered Charity Schools in general: their endowments—particularly if they increase in value—always become objects of peculation to trustees and governors, and in the end the greater portion is subverted from the original purpose of the investments; schools turned into close boroughs, that there’s no poking any little snub-nose into without some great influence, like a hydraulic ram at his back;—mostly used for rich men’s illegitimates, keeping fatheaded deacons in greasy sinecures, and a few unfortunate boys in a fantastic dress, that makes them ashamed of themselves for the rest of their lives.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (R.) Ha, ha! very true. Such has often been the issue of charitable bequests; but rest assured the Hawthorne property will not be disposed of in that manner.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: (L.) Glad of it—was sure of it;—wrote to Cunningham that all was right, and have been over to the Bishopric myself, and obtained a special license for the marriage of Francis Hawthorne and Lucy Vavasour, in the little parish church that neighbours the entrance to the park of the Woodlands.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p:
That was premature; our formal consent to an
union should have preceded, and would have sufficed for the present.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Your formal consent goes for nothing without the marriage, as the will is worded: thought of that; and as you are the only man to whom I don’t object, resolved to look after your interest in time.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Well, well, I thank you, and it matters but little.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
Frank.
sp.p: Yes; no.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: I object emphatically to such a contradictory reply. People should know their own minds.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (R.) Doctor, I have faith in you. Your very eccentricity, if a little gnarled, is still a natural growth. There is no need with you to exact a promise—to set the seal of silence upon a confidence.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Well, I don’t object to that.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: I will confess to you that I did love, and still could love, Lucy—Miss Vavasour; that I did admire, respect, even when most consciously the object of her scorn; but that now I cannot give my esteem, nay, have some difficulty in withholding my contempt, from the woman who seems base enough to give her heart to a title, and yet sell her person for wealth.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: (L.) I object to an argument founded like yours on assumption. Lucy’s not so bad; accustomed all her life to wealth, she must look upon it as a necessity.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (R., sighs) True.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: (L.) She has seen so many sacrifices of feeling made for its preservation or acquirement, thinks it not a crime, but a custom; then it is so mixed up, or rather baited with sentimental lures, tacked cleverly on by affected willing victims, and humbugging old mothers—bleeding hearts, sleepless nights, pearly tears, bliss of secret agony, romance of a strife between duty and inclination, no wonder a young girl, not quite sure of her own mind, should rather prefer to be a victim than otherwise.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p:
Doctor, doctor, this is special pleading. The cant of the nursery, or of the boudoir, should neither find utterance on the man’s lips, nor echo in the woman’s heart. No, no; romance may heighten, beautify with its tinted light the sacrifices made for truth’s sake, even as the hues of the stained window serve to enrich the twilight atmosphere of the cloister, or to soften the sunbeam on the cenotaph; but you must not take it into the service of self-interest, or make it pander to the legalization of vice, and hope to preserve its loveliness, or to make its charms a plea for sinning. Romance risks poverty, adventures peril, to wed hands with hearts, even as natural alliances are made among the simplest, least sophisticated, and
so most civilised of the civilised races of men; and, wanting the grand element of a natural instinct, no romance is worth the telling or the hearing, how much less, then, the acting? (crosses to L.)

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Can’t object to that; he always gets the better of me in an argument.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: But dismiss your fears on my account; with moderate desires I can live, even without the annuity accruing to me, should I refuse a joint share in this princely inheritance.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: (R.) But you are not going to refuse? I object to that. You owe it to your uncle’s and father’s memory to keep it in the name of Hawthorne.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: My dear doctor, what I shall do as yet, short as is the time, is a secret to myself. The sequel you will know almost as soon as I shall. (Doctor goes up R.)

div.stage: Enter Sir Lionel Norman, R.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Gentlemen, good day. Mr. Hawthorne, if you are not particularly engaged, I should feel obliged by a few minutes’ private conversation.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Certainly, if Dr. Playfair will give me leave.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Oh! I don’t object to that. (aside) Never shall, to get out of that over-refined gentleman’s company.

div.stage: Exit, L.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (L.) Now, Sir Lionel, I am at your service.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (R.) Mr. Hawthorne, I must beg your allowance of the liberty I am about to take, which courtesy, even under peculiar circumstances, can alone excuse. I come to speak upon a very delicate subject, and one that requires the nicest treatment. In fact, the suggestion I am about to make could only be volunteered from one gentleman to another. (Frank bows) It is, that you will not force upon Miss Vavasour the marriage prescribed by the will.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (aside) Delicate with a vengeance. I do not. She is at liberty to refuse me.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Oh, yes; but not without an enormous sacrifice of wealth.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Which enormous sacrifice she has sent you to suggest that I should make?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Oh, no; she is not aware that I have interfered in the matter.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Then why do you?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Why out of consideration for her, as she does not love you—In fact—ahem!—may be presumed to have long loved another.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: What a delicate gentleman! (aside) She has said as much?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: No, not exactly; but it is an inference—a fact to be implied from the whole tenor of her conduct.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p:
Indeed! I had thought, Sir Lionel, that the sacrifice
she is expected to make—I mean, of course, not of wealth, but of feeling—affection, was not unusual in fashionable life.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Well, neither is it; but as you are a new man, and seem to have stricter principles in these matters, I thought your early prejudices in favour of the opposite kind of sacrifice might induce you—

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: To make myself the victim, and enable Miss Vavasour to become Lady Norman, and Sir Lionel Norman lord of the Woodlands! Upon my word, Baronet, you compliment me so highly by your generous and chivalric opinion, that I should be only too happy to oblige you. But, pray why did not this suggestion come from Mrs. Vavasour, who must have her daughter’s confidence in the matter.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Oh, because she did not think you likely to be persuaded; and, believing that the marriage must take place, she was fearful it might prejudice her daughter’s future peace of mind, should it provoke your anger.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Oh, she does not hold so high an opinion of me as you do. I am again your debtor, Sir Lionel, but at present can hold out no hope of compliance with this particular request. It is too late; wants but an hour or so of the appointed time. Egad, I must go and dress. You will be present at the ceremony, of course?

div.stage: Exit, L. 2 E.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Um! I gave him credit for more high principle: the mercantile spirit of the age has depraved even the scholar and the puritan.

div.stage: Exit into library, L.
Enter Lucy, with scroll of music paper, and Jenny, R.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: And are you sure—quite sure—that Mr. Hawthorne is the author of these words?

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Am I sure? Am I a clever little girl?—Listen and judge. Yesterday, passing one of the rooms upon our side— the Montague side of the mansion—I caught the sounds of a piano; I entered, and found my cousin Romeo, I mean Frank, playing from a new score, and humming, rather than singing, the words. He was confused, apologised for having remained, and when I enquired his theme, said it was an old strain he had been endeavouring to arrange to some new words, but as the work was imperfect, he declined to submit it to my judgment. In his confusion, and the haste of his exit, he did not notice that on the table he had left a copy—the first, I should presume, from the erasures. I found it, liked, and thought it expressed a feeling that you ought to know.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Is he not yet conscious of its being in your possession?

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p:
I don’t know—I have not seen him since; he is very sensitive, and if he has discovered its loss, would rather slight
its importance, by not seeming to care about it, than suggest its having been the echo of a real feeling by an enquiry.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Yes ; that is in keeping with his character.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: (r.) Oh ! you have taken some notes of that. Have I guessed rightly in believing that you have learned at last to feel a little interest about this self-willed cousin of mine?

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: The peculiarities of his character are too apparent to escape notice, even had not the relation in which we stand compelled some observance.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: That is said in the very spirit of a grand lady ; but the grand lady has a heart in her bosom, as well as the little girl; and yours, which I am sure has not been vitiated, must be susceptible of a nearer interest than can possibly belong to mere observance about the character of your future husband.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (l.) My future husband—who knows ?

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: (r.) Love ever suggests liking in return, and he loves you.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Nonsense, child. He dreamt of an Egeria,—an ideal mistress—but, looking on the idol, found it false-—very dross and clay. Love me !—he makes no effort to win my love—he moves, speaks, and thinks, independently of all reference to me. No, no ; he accepts me as his destiny—hopeful, perhaps, that at the last moment I may refuse. It is but a fair retort; I did the same, and earned his contempt, and my own, too. {(seats herself at L. table)} I know that, but he shouldn't show it so plainly—wouldn't, if he had a spark of love. Pretty wooing.

Jenny, (aside) It is excellent. I believe that he has acted in the very best way to win your love ; his instinct is almost as shrewd as my skill, clever as I know myself, (to Lucy) And, so you don't love him? It's a sad fate to look forward to, and you might have been so happy with half the fortune, and Sir Lionel Norman.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Sir Lionel?—-perhaps.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: You can sing at sight; I wish you'd just try over that song for me.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: No, no; I—I couldn't.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Do, do; see, here is a piano; let me accompany you; it's very pretty; just listen, (plays symphony) Pray, try the words over.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: No, no. Pshaw—it's very childish.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Then please give it back to me, as you don't like it, and I do.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
Jenny.
sp.p:
{(aside)} If you do I shall become a nun, which I am too clever a little girl, and know my vocation too well, ever to
meditate. I shall be in attendance as your bridesmaid before the ceremony takes place; at present mamma may want me.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p:
The ceremony—will it ever take place ? And are these his real sentiments, or is it a mere poetical fancy ? There is an air of truth in the very simplicity of the words ; they are not brain wrought, but heart sung : should I reject him, 'twere noble; but then, how to convince him of my motive, (crosses to R. table) Love should cling unto the faintest hope. Let me try to sing this song ; it may reveal, as music often does, the true pulse of my heart, (seats herself at piano—plays and sings)
Song.
Though beauteously blossoms the myrtle tree,
And the laurel a nobler shrub may be,
Oh ! the willow's the plant that most likes me,
For in spite of thy scorn I still love thee,
Aye, more than thy bridegroom, whoe'er he may be ;
Sing hey! ho ! willow—green willow for me.
Come, kiss me, despair, and new life impart,
Take mine, and to me give thy bold, brave heart,
To endure, not deny, the deep, deep pain,
And yet anger none at her high disdain;
For love unto pride can a true love be;
Sing, hey ! ho ! willow—green willow for me.
Though one glance of love from thy glorious eyes
Far richer made me than the wealth you prize,
Twere better from thee in thy scorn to part,
Than to win one false smile, and abase thy heart;
Oh! no: in thy pride I can still love thee ;
Sing, hey! ho ! willow—green willow for me. (at end bursts into tears; rises, and crosses to L., then back to R.) Weeping ? fool! I care neither for him nor for his money ; let him keep it; I don't want it. Pshaw! he contemns me—can't love. Shall I stoop to woo his respect ? Never !

div.stage: Enter Sir Lionel Norman, L. 3 E.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Pardon, Miss Vavasour, if that I intrude. I have been speaking seriously to your mother, and the result is a desire to do my duty honourably by you.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Sir Lionel, one word first. I have not the remotest claim upon your consideration or duty, and perhaps this assurance will spare you further avowal.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: {(aside)} Strange !—can't mean it; angry because I have not spoken before. Pardon me; I may have seemed absent, cold of late.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: I have not noticed it, and fully pardon that which gave me no concern.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (aside, L.) Still wrath? Well, Miss Vavasour, I rejoice to hear you say this, because you will have less difficulty in accomplishing the painful but necessary sacrifice of feeling you are required to make.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: What sacrifice ?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: That of your feelings to your interest.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: What feelings?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (aside)} She wants an eclaireissement in the hope of evoking the spirit of the past, and inducing us both to make fools of ourselves.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: You do not reply.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: It is useless to particularise feelings that must of necessity be laid aside, and buried with the past.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (R.) I really don't understand you.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (L.) Well, then, Miss Vavasour, it is my duty honourably to inform you, that much as I esteem, truly as I admire, have loved, and still love you, were you to resign this fortune out of regard to the past, it would not be in my power to atone in some part for the sacrifice; and therefore, in a spirit of self-abnegation, I set you free. I advise you—

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: To marry another man, whom you think I do not like ; and yet you talk of respecting—loving me in the same breath. Oh, shame ! Oh, Chivalry, thou hast indeed fled the earth, and Mammon has established his empire over the heart of youth itself, in peer and peasant, (crosses to R. corner)

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Pardon me, it is not for myself I speak, it is from a consideration of your welfare.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Which you think more likely to be assured by a fortune than by your love. That is probable ; but I will give you more credit than you do yourself, Sir Lionel: your advice commits no outrage upon the character of your love, which could not co-exist in the same breast with the feelings that prompted your honourable consideration for my welfare. You never loved me, nor I you, of that I have been long convinced : we moved in the same society, went to the same balls, danced in the same set of quadrilles, applauded the same operas, and being equally young and heartless, thought we were a pair, but we were not; and this act, which completes, or rather establishes, the fact of our severance, does not cost the slightest pang to either.— Good day, sir. Exit, R.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p:
(C.) Mere spleen and vexation. I expected an explosion. Poor thing, she'll go and cry now. I'm sorry for her —sorry to have lost her, for I really liked her; but the man who has the dignity of a baronet to sustain upon a mortgaged estate, and who marries a poor lady, is little less than a scoundrel: because, if he should have children, they must not work, and
they must live, so he becomes the father of a race of genteel sharpers—otherwise fortune hunters.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Sir Lionel, I am fortunate in finding you, as from your professed friendship, I am sure of your advice on what is to me a very important subject.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Miss Wildbriar, I am most honoured by your confidence. Command every service in my power to render you.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: You have so often noticed me, and always with so much kindness, that not having any one in my own family who is familiar with high life, or capable of advising me, I thought of you the moment I was asked to become a marchioness.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: A marchioness!

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Yes; and I said to myself, Sir Lionel, who is the very pattern of high life, the beau ideal of a fine gentleman, can best tell me whether or not I ought to accept this offer— whether I could hope to move in the fashionable world without provoking a question as to my birth and breeding.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (L.) Upon that subject, let me, my dear Miss Wildbriar, give you the best proof of my most favourable opinion, by myself soliciting the honour and happiness of your alliance.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: You, Sir Lionel—you—why, I always thought you were in love with Miss Vavasour.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Miss Wildbriar, I shall be candid with you, and own that for a long time I was devoted to that lady—was indeed— until your star rose over the horizon, and drew the eye of my affection after it; doubtless with more ease, because the terms of the will made it a duty, upon my part, to avert my thoughts from Miss Vavasour.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Oh, dear me ! I wish I had known this sooner; for as a baronet is not so high up as a marquis, I think I should not be so likely to lose my self-command and stumble ; and the worst of it is, I have given my promise.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Promise ! This is really too bad, Miss Wildbriar. Why come to ask my advice as to what you should do, when you have already decided your fate?

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: (R.) Isn't that what people always do? But don't be angry; mine was not a formal promise, it was only the half consent, that not saying " no," with women, particularly young women is, it is mostly taken for an affirmative, but they can always deny having said "yes" afterwards, if necessary. And are you really serious, Sir Lionel, in—in your proposal ?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Miss Wildbriar, I should else be worse than a scoundrel—a puppy !

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: And you would make me mistress of that dear delightful place, Norman Abbey ?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: And of its master, too,

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: (R.) Well, then—oh! but I forgot, and perhaps you did. My mother and brother—what am I to do with them ?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Miss Wildbriar, as your mother and brother, I should respect them, at a distance. I must be candid. I could not introduce them into my circle.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Oh! and I should never see them?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Nay, I should not object to your visiting, and passing a day or so with them once a year.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: But they might never come to Norman Abbey ?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (L.) Certainly not, on account of the servants' remarks.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Don't you think, Sir Lionel, that all circumstances considered, it were better for you not to risk any connection with a family you regard as likely to bring you into discredit even with your servants ?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Oh, no. The beauty and manners of Lady Norman are certain to approve Sir Lionel Norman's taste, and the wife's individuality is merged in the husband's. Not so with her family. I should marry you, not the others.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Not though each of them was dowered with £100,000?

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: No.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Well, Sir Lionel, your honesty has a particular merit on the part of a very fine gentleman, and compels a like return of honest avowal upon my part. I would not marry a marquis who was ashamed either of me or mine. From the first moment of our acquaintance, I have seen that my mother and brother's provincialities were subjects of scorn and of ridicule to you, and to your clique, and yet each one of us has been tolerated and flattered in private, as the great people's interest suggested. While making love to Miss Vavasour you coquetted with me, and I confess that I did the like with you, for the purpose of leading you on to this proposal, and of paying back the slights put, both covertly and openly, on plain, but honest people whose possessions are coveted by fine, but disingenuous gentlemen. Sir Lionel Norman, the little Lancashire farmer's daughter begs to decline the honour of your alliance.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (L.) In the hope of becoming a marchioness.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Oh, no: I suspected that jest the moment I heard it, and almost immediately satisfied myself by reference to a directory, which told me there was no such peer as the Marquis of Banterdown, but that there was a Mr. Everard Digby, barrister.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Well, Miss Wildbriar, I am in your hands. You have gained your point; you triumph, and I seek no mercy.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p:
Spoken like a hero that deserves some. I am mute Sir Lionel, from this moment, as to the past; and as I think Miss Vavasour did once like you, and may be induced again and as my cousin is a strange man, let me advise you to keep
your own counsel, remain here, and wait the issue; who knows, the fortune may be yours yet.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: Thank you, my good fairy. (crosses, R.) But I cannot, with any grace of gentlemanly feeling, meditate its acquirement now. Your servant. Exit, R.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Poor Sir Lionel! I think he will give over coquetry from to-day.

div.stage: Enter James, L.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: She his {h}alone at last. I thought the baronite was about to {h}anticipate me, but, from the 'igh tones in which they spoke, there can have been no proposal. Miss Wildbriar, may I 'ave the {h}extreme 'appiness of {h}addressing a word to you?

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Mr. James, is it you? Certainly. What is it? A message from Miss Vavasour?

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: No, Miss Wildbriar; it's a message from my 'art; it's a personal communication, which you will be pleased to consider marked private and strictly confidential.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Dear me, you surprise me ; what is its nature ?

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Miss Wildbriar, let me ask you, hare you {h}aware of my {h}antecedents, and of the 'igh position I 'ave {h}ever held as a {h}upper servant in the most recherchiest establishments in Belgravia and May Fair ?

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Well, no, Mr. James.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: What's Jenny talking to that flunky for? I'll listen.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Miss Wildbriar, I {h}am {h}of a particularly sensitive nature, {h}and I meditate retiring into private life. I am proud to say not without resources. I have an account at Coutts', and credit for three oughts with a very respectable numeral before them.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: I am glad to hear it; your's will be a dignified retirement.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: I 'ope it will; but I 'ave all my life been accustomed to society, and I hope to be accompanied {h}into privacy by a companion of the softer sex.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Oh, you are going to be married, I presume to Miss Jemima Simperton ?

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Oh, no ; I {h}am {h}aware that there is a softness in that quarter, but I have had a glimpse into her bank-book, and the figure don't suit.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Then who is to be the happy woman?

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: (L.) That's a home question, and brings us to the point. The 'appy woman—and 'appy she may be, as I {h}offer her my 'and, and my 'art—a werry unusual circumstance with {h}uppers—the 'appy woman I sees before me.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: In your mind's eye, I presume ?

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: I {h}am not {h}aware that my mind has any {h}eye. No ! I sees her now with the {h}eyes in my 'ead.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: What, me ? Oh, this is too good.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Oh, no; I don't want to make a merit of the condescension. I have but one objection to make, that his to the other members of your family, whom I should {h}expect you, as soon as the settlements are made, and your fortune secured tightly, to cut altogether.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Kind and condescending, Mr. James—even more exacting than the baronet. Oh ! if mamma could only hear this. Mr. James, upon this subject I must refer you to mamma.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: (falls on both knees) I should prefer your breaking the matter to her first; has there is a {h}absence of sympathy between us, she might object.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: (coming forward, C.) I should think she would ; why, you brazen-faced impudent flunky, to dare to think my daughter, wi' her edication and a hundred thousand pounds, would demean hersen to wed wi' the loikes o' thee. Jenny, you ought to be ashamed of yoursen to talk to him at all.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: I perceive this must be a case of Gretna Green, Miss Wildbriar, if it is to come off at {h}all.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Ha! ha ! ha !

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Be off wi' you, or I'll Gretna Green thee. I shall forget mysen if thee doesn't go, and shake all the flower out of your powdered nob.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: {H}assaults are {h}actionable.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: (seated, C.) I dunnot care; I shouldn't mind payin' for the pleasure of beatin' thee. Away wi' you, my hands are itching to serve thee out.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: (L. C.) In that case I had better go.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Do, Mr. James, and preserve the dignity of an upper, in the calm contemplation of a great mistake.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: That's very pointed; she rejects and turns me into reticule—sneers at me, too. (sings a line or two of "Love Not," in broken voice — burlesque style. Mrs. Wildbriar rises, comes down, and threatens him) Oh ! {h}agony !—oh ! {h}agony ! Exit, L.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: (L.) Oh! this is delightful.—Ha! ha! ha!

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: For shame of thysen for laughing at, or listening to, him at all; thou ought have boxed his ears, and thee going to wed wi' a marquis.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Oh, dear no, mamma; truth must out: Mr. Everard Digby is no more than a barrister.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Aye, to be sure; that's his cognito.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p:
Ha ! ha ! mamma, the fiction of his being a marquis
was only a stupid jest, put upon you by Mr. Adolphus, and one to which Everard was never privy.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Eh! have they all been laughing at me for my mistakes. Well, they may call themselves ladies and gentlemen, but they couldn't do more if they were Bolton trotters; but never mind, I'm not so foolish as to make them laugh more by losing my temper. Thee shall wed wi' him all the same ; and may be it's all the better, for thou might'st make a very good wife, wench, to a plain gentleman, and be but a poor catch for a marquis.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Oh, mamma! a marquis can be no more than a gentleman. But you are right, I might falter, and trip under the weight of a coronet and an ermined robe; for I'm only a Lancashire wild rose, after all.Exit, R. 1 E.

div.stage: Enter Robin, L.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: (L.) Where's my mother ?—oh, she's here.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: (R.) What is it you want, lad ?

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Well, you see, there's a lot of folks going to be wed. Jenny and her cognito—that's either a lawyer or a lord—I don't know which; and cousin and Miss Lucy; and so, ecod, 1 think I may as well have a turn mysen.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: What do you mean ?

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Why, I wants to get wed, too.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Go on wi' you—it's too soon yet, wi' your smock face, to think of getting wed.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Why, I have as much right as Jenny, and more, if it comes to that; for I'm older, by a matter of two years.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: That's nought to signify. The lad should always be a deal older than the lass. I don't mind thee flirting and sweet-hearting a bit, for lads will do it—it's only natural; but thee munnot think of getting wed these four or five years, when thee'lt look summat like a man.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Get off wi' ye! I mun not ?

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: No ; you mun not.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Then all fat's i' th' fire; and you mun pay damages for breach of promise.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Why; what hast done ?

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Why, I ha' done it.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: What hast done?

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Why, I dun it—axed lass to marry.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Well, she'll take no harm o' that, and won't die of fright.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p:
No ; but you told me I might gie her a buss; and when I met her again, and tried to, she wouldn't let me, and said that it was only proper between folks as wur going to wed; and so I up and axed her to wed wi' me; and she seemed struck of a heap, and couldn't answer; and that Mr, Adolphus coming
in overheard me, and advised her to take me, and told her I wur a good match—the only civil word he ever said before— and that she mightn't have such a chance again; and I said that he was right, for I wouldn't ax her or anybody else twice over.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: That was right enough, and straight forward.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Well, then, she said summut about it's only being a joke, and that I wasn't in earnest; and Mr. Adolphus told me I mun write a letter, promising to marry her; and he wrote out a form, in which I promised to wed her right off, or forfeit £5,000; and I signed, and gie'd it lass, and she took it, but wouldn't gie me a buss then, till she saw you, and had your consent.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Eh ! you soft one—I'll consent her—where is she?

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: There, outside the door, waiting till I call her in, to get your blessing.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Call her in. I'll give her a blessing.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: No, will you? It's all right, then—come in, lass, mother consents.
sp.stage: Enter Miss Euphemia, L. 1 E. Robin puts her across to C.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Come here, and look me in the face ; thou shameless baggage, to go and take advantage of a poor, soft, ninny loike that lad, and hook him into a promise. For shame o' thysen; but thou shan't have him, nor touch a penny o' my money. I'll spend it all, to the last fraction, sooner than thou should'st get a farthing.

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: (C.) What is the meaning of this?

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: To come over lad wi' your sly ways, and your coaxing looks, and your humming and hawing, and all the time you don't care the snap of my finger for him, and only want to get a tight hould of his bit o' money.

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: Oh ! this is too much, (bursts into tears, crosses to L .)

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: (C.) Eh! mother, dunnot be too hard on lass; see, thee'st made her cry.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: (R.) Let her cry—she's only shamming—they can cry when they like—she's laughin' in her sleeve; but let her bundle out of my presence, or I'll put my mark on her—I will.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Na, na, mother, (crosses to C.) Dom me, if I stand it any longer. I'm my own measter, and I'll wed wench if I loike, and if I don't I'll go to sea, or list for a sodger, and I'll never see thee never no more; and you may gie all the money to Jenny. I'll earn my bread for mysen, for you're none behaving to me like a man—like a woman, I mean—nor a mother to me.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Eh! that's more than lad ever said to me before.

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p:
Oh, Mr. Wildbriar, you should not make use of such language to your mother on any account. Don't let me be the cause of strife. Forget me—I make no claim—have
no right. Be ruled by your mother, she is the best guide for you, for no one can have your interest so much at heart.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: And he talks of never seeing me, never no more, and all on account of you.

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: He does not mean it; as for me, I shall not be the cause of dispute—I resign my hopes—I give him back his promise, (crosses to C.)

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: No, will you—the written one?

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: What written one ?

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Haw! haw! haw! (crosses to C.) Eh! mother, didst think I was so soft, and I born in Lancashire? I wanted to try lass, and I offered to give her bond ; she didn't know I was only trotting her, Bolton fashion ; but she wouldn't take it.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Not take bond for £5,000!

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: No !

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: No ! for by refusing five, I made sure of a hundred.(aside)

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Nor give me a promise without your leave, and then I made up my mind to have her; but I thought it best to trot you a bit first, and I have done it nicely, too. Haw ! haw! haw !

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: (R.) Eh! I dunnot mind, sin you were not so soft as I thought. Gie me a buss, lad, thou'rt thy mother's son.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: And my feyther's, too.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Come here, lass, thou'rt a good wench. (Robin passes her to C.) And if you'll only wait five or six years ——

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Get off wi' ye, five or six years !

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Well, then, a year or so; thou shalt have him, one hundred thousand pounds, and my blessing.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: (L.) Hooray! hooray! And now mother and I'll both have a buss.

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Aye, that we will. (kisses her) Come along, lass, and let's have a bit talk together, and get acquainted like. Thou'rt a bonny lass, too !

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Eh ! that she is, or she might ha' whistled for me.

div.stage: Frank Hawthorne, L., dressed as for wedding, and Mrs. Vavasour, R., enter from different sides)

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: I have been informed, that you wish to speak to me, madam.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: Yes, Mr. Hawthorne; our mutual position, on the verge of a very close relationship, must excuse the freedom and confidential character of my speech. You appear to have taken umbrage at the little device Lucy and I put in practice in the early stage of our acquaintance with you, the motive of which was rather to elicit your character than to offend you.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: You are quite sure of that?

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: (R.) Well, I think—I'm sure that such was Lucy's idea.

sp.speaker: Frank.
