sp.p: (aside) It is not true; but on the woman's lips we forgive the white lie the mother's heart suggests.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: Now, although previously Lucy may have had other prospects, of course she has of late looked forward only to her union with you, and has endeavoured to adapt herself to your ideas and views; and it has pained me to observe that you have not seemed to appreciate her amiability in this matter.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: I thought the compliment was paid rather to the future fortune than to the future husband.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: Oh, Mr. Hawthorne, you do yourself and my daughter, too, an injustice. Of course the fortune is an object —a great object—but we must have some little consideration for the husband as well.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (aside)} Extraordinary condescension !

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: Now, Mr. Hawthorne, Lucy has noticed your neglect, and, I perceive, feels it deeply. I have seen traces of tears in her eyes even this morning, and I wish that just before you depart for the church, or sign the settlements, you would say a few gentle words to Lucy, which will assuage her fears of not being liked by you, and give a little grace to the sacrifice she has made of all former views.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (L.— aside ) Sacrifice! still sacrifice, and yet this plea. You think it possible, then, that otherwise, she might refuse me at the last moment ?

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: (aside)} Refuse! he mustn't think that. Refuse! oh, dear, no; she likes you too well for that, she must obey the will, she had always such a respect for your uncle ; his wish was law with her, and there is so much dependent on it, to refuse would be madness; but she might show grief, perhaps burst into tears at the altar, and that would be such a scene— so painful, you know. A few kind words from you will spare us that, if you will be only good enough to speak them beforehand.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Well, madam, I shall think the matter over.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: Oh, I am sure that you will oblige me; it will so comfort and encourage my poor child. Exit, R.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: How anxious this woman is to rose over the sacrifice of her child's feelings; what tender care, what a delicate consideration, she has for appearances; and with what cold-blooded determination she puts the knife into the breast of a first affection, that murmurs rebellion against the sway of self-interest! With what surgical indifference she cuts off the superfluous growth of passion, and smiles the while the patient writhes and groans! And yet she thinks herself a good mother, and is really acting and speaking under the instinct of a fond one. It is the Mammon worship that has perverted and corrupted in her breast that purest and most holy of all instincts, a mother's love, into a worldly policy, and made it a blight, and not a beauty—a curse, and not a blessing. (crosses to r,)

div.stage: Enter Dr. Playfair and Mr. Cunningham, L.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: (L.) Here is old Punctuality as usual, not a minute before or after his time.

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: (crosses to C.) Ah, Mr. Hawthorne, good day ; glad to see you; particularly as you are—toilet complete, exact to your appointment, and ready for the occasion. I congratulate you, sir, upon this business-like self-possession at such a moment; marriage and inheritance—a lady wife, and a large fortune— very great trials for the nerves, as prosperity is harder to bear with equanimity even than adversity.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (aside) Yes, for I am cool and calm, with the catastrophe of an epic foreshadowed on my heart.(crosses to L. table)

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: Where are the other parties ?—no time to waste; must put in appearance before twelve o'clock; just eleven hours three minutes and fifteen seconds of the day gone ;—it will take fifteen minutes to sign settlements, &c, and another quarter of an hour to reach the church.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: I object to that—only ten minutes, as the carriages are ready at the door.

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: Tut! we must make allowance for the ladies, shawling and bonneting, sure to waste time.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: I don't object to that; they always do. (at L. table) I sent word that you had arrived, and, see, our friends are here.
sp.stage: Enter Sir Lionel Norman, Hon. Augustus Adolphus, Mrs. Vavasour, Jenny, and Lucy, R., dressed as for bridal —Everard Digby, Mrs. Wildbriar, Robin, Jenny, and Miss Euphemia, L. ; Jenny crosses L. to Lucy.

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: Good day, good day, ladies and gentlemen. You are all aware that this is the last hour of the six months prescribed for determining the recipients or application of the large amount of the late Mr. Hawthorne's property, under his will, formally read by me. Here are two forms of assent and dissent, for Lucy Vavasour and Francis Hawthorne, either of which they will please to sign within the five minutes (looks at watch) that I can spare for a reconsideration of their previous determination, (hands two forms to Lucy, ditto to Frank— Frank rises)

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: (up, C.) What nonsense ! as if either would be so soft as to refuse.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p:
(up, L.) Cousin won't, I hope : but no fear ; he was
brought up in Lancashire, and he's got his head screwed on right way.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: (who has crossed to Frank, C.) Oh, Mr. Hawthorne, won't you say a kind word to Lucy ?

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: No, madam; your daughter's feelings, or her policy, must direct her choice. I shall not light one false beacon on her path, nor cast the lure of one persuasive word to tempt her into infamy.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: Well, well, don't speak so loud, she might hear you.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: (L., to Frank : rises)} Remember, if you make a fool of yourself, and refuse, I shall object to you all my life. (seats himself again)

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: Oh ! Lucy! Lucy! don't refuse; don't rush into poverty. I'm sure he'll be a kind man, and let you have your own way.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Heaven grant that now he may prove a harsh one.— But, hush! mother, hush ! I am listening to my heart.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: (R.) Oh ! don't—don't ! listen to anything but that;—listen to your mother's prayers and entreaties.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (R.) He's watching us ;—his mind is resolved. I know—I know his thought. Ah ! a gleam of hope ! I see the way. Yes, mother, yes ! I, too, have resolved.
sp.stage: ( embraces her mother, Frank shrugs his shoulders: she sits down to table, R.; hesitates for a moment ; signs one of the papers, and then buries her face in her hands on table)

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: ,(l , aside)} She has signed the consent—I am sure of it. Yes, the struggle was fierce, but short, and Mammon has prevailed, (goes to sign, L.)

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Mind what you're about, lad; and don't sign wrong paper.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (R., aside to Sir Lionel) If he should, the mortgages upon the baronetcy would soon be liquidated.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Tis done!
sp.stage: (Dr. Playfair takes paper to Cunningham, and returns to seat, L. C. )

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: Mr. Hawthorne—Miss Vavasour—the five minutes have expired. The assentforms that you have signed, if you please.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (to her mother) Take it—give it—I cannot!

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (at table, L.) Some shame left still; even that's a virtue—a poor one—only to be prized from all the others' absence.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: Here, here, Mr. Cunningham, is my daughter's— good child. I am so happy !

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p:
(reads at table, C.) " I, Lucy Vavasour, do hereby " affirm, declare, and make known, to the executors and trustees, " under the will of the late George Hawthorne, and to all parties {"} having an interest therein, that I accept the condition attached " to a joint inheritance with Francis Hawthorne, under said
" will; and agree to contract the bonds of legal marriage with " the said Francis Hawthorne, on this day, January 6th, 1858. " Witness my hand and seal—Lucy Vavasour."

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: (up C.) Good lass, and a happy wedding to thee!

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: (up C. L.) And many of them !

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Ha! ha! ha!

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Eh ! the lad thinks a wedding like New Year or Christmas day.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Hush ! mother, hush !

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: (reads)} " I, Francis Hawthorne, do hereby affirm, " declare, and make known, to the executors and trustees under " the will of the late George Hawthorne, and to all parties "having an interest therein, that I " Ahem! eh ! bless my soul! Have you mistaken the document, sir?

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: No.

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: (reads) " That I decline to accept the conditions " attached to a joint inheritance with Lucy Vavasour, under " said will." (Lucy starts up)

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Why, he's gied her all the property.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: (L.) Eh ! the d—d fool !

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: (reads) " And hereby formally and finally refuse to contract a marriage with the said Lucy Vavasour. Witness my hand and seal—Francis Hawthorne."

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (rushing forward, and falling on her knees) Thank Heaven! (R. C.)

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: (rises, L. C.) Aye ! thank Heaven that I have had more pity on thee than thon hadst upon thyself; that I have spared thee the equal sin and torture of an unholy marriage— the life of wretchedness that must have ensued from wedding one your heart abhorred.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (R. c, rising) Sir?

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Aye, look angrily ; it had become thee better but a moment before. Listen—harken all—my motive for this deed, not from vain-glory do I publish it, but to vindicate the act from any suspicion of recklessness, or want of due consideration. I did admire—did love this woman. I have no shame in the avowal, even when I knew she loved me not. I might have continued to love her, had she continued to make plain her known dislike, but tempted by this mighty wealth, she made a bargain with her pride—her heart—to hide themselves, till she had grasped some part of the golden prize.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Indeed, sir?

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p:
Indeed, indeed. What, then, remained? The sacrifice of wealth— vast wealth, 'tis true, and almost fabulous, or the sacrifice of honesty and self-esteem, the double sacrifice between us —man's honor, woman's purity—those first great principles, in which consists the soul's true likeness to its Maker—more vast than time or space—the true illimitable—the eternity ! I could
not—did not hesitate, and have no pride of what I've done, save what accrues from simple self-respect preserved. I am accustomed to an humble life, can live it contentedly; you could not; and in a worldly point of view, the lesser forfeiture is mine. May it lift thee to the full height of the generous nobleness that often springs from vast possession, never doomed to companionship with pettiness of thought, a little meanness, or a wrangling envy.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: (R.) Oh, this is truly noble—truly generous.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: He's a great character.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: (up, R.) Accept our thanks.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Spare thanks—acknowledgments. I do not reciprocate your esteem. I have no wish to be rude, but henceforth we are strangers. (crosses L. to Doctor, and then goes up stage)

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: (comes down, C.) He's the man for my money. He shall have my daughter Jenny.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Hush ! No, no, mamma. (coming down)

sp.speaker: Everard.
sp.p: Second cousins can't marry, you know. (jestingly)

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Then I'll have him mysen, if he'll have an old woman and £50,000.

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Get off, and sit down, do. Shut up, mother!

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (to Frank, who has shaken hands with Dr. Playfair, and is going) Stay, sir, one moment, if you please; we have had too much of double-dealing and stage-play. I would escape all further deception—run no risk of a snare. Is this document really formal and sufficient, Mr Cunningham ?

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: It needs but our attestation as witnesses, that we have seen him sign. (Frank comes down L. C.)

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: Let that be done, and, pray, in the gentleman's presence. (Dr. Playfair and Cunningham sign) And now you tell me, in your character of executors and trustees, that this makes me mistress of all—that the whole of the fortune belongs to me—to me alone.

sp.speaker: Dr. P. and Mr. C.
sp.p: It does.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (crossing to Frank) Then I shall wear the willow all my life, and have it planted on my grave, if you a second time refuse me.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: How?

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: (holding up scroll) I know by these dear words, (shows song) you love me still; and I am thine—thine, heart and soul, and have been long.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Oh, heaven! Can this be true, or is it a cruel jest ?

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: I knew your doubts; I guessed your proud resolve. Had I refused, all had been lost. This mighty wealth, your just inheritance, I made but mine to give it with myself to thee,

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Lucy, (they embrace) God bless thee!

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Hurray ! hurray ? Lancashire for ever !

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Silence !—No, I don't object to that. Hurrah !

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Who cares if thee did, man ? The lad's our cousin, and lass mun be our cousin now, and I'm glad on it, for she's best in house.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: It was my device that brought you together—taught you to know each other. You owe all your happiness to me.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: I object to that, doctor. My cousin will own to having taken a hint from a clever little girl, and Lucy must admit being in my debt for a glimpse into her lover's heart.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: No, no; I will not admit being in debt to any one but to my husband, to whom I owed all my heart three months ago.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: My love had still an earlier date ; it sprang into existence with my first glimpse of thee.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: There's ingratitude!—what selfish creatures lovers are!

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: I object to that, or you and I would mind our own business.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Why you are not in love, are you? With whom?

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: I object to telling you, or anybody else.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Never mind, I shall find out, as sure as I'm a clever little girl. I suspect already it's with my mother.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: No, no, no; I object to that.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Ha! ha ! ha! that's the very reason I believe it. Ha ! ha ! mamma, will you have the doctor for a beau ?

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: Noa, I should object to that; no, thank you; if I wanted to make a fool of mysen, I should have a better excuse for it than a cranky old body like that.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (R.) The poor baronetcy has lost its chance of escape from the usurious clutch of the mortgagees.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: But the baronet has received a lesson that has raised him above participation in such regrets. Mr. Hawthorne, I trust you will this time accept the congratulations on your great and deserved felicity, which I most sincerely proffer.

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Manfally and generously spoken, Sir Lionel, and in the true spirit of that chivalry which has ever been, and I trust ever will be, the prevailing characteristic of an English gentleman. ( shakes hands)} And hark ye, Messrs. Executors, if not upon so great a scale as suggested by the codicil, still we shall have the school; for to be a man of the day, one must have regard to the interests of the coming man.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: (rises and comes to R. C.) Mr. Hawthorne, if you should feel disposed to extend your investments —

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: Unfortunately, I have no overstock of modesty or of common sense, or, in charity to your need, I should lend you some, Mr. Adolphus.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: I should object to your lending him anything. Both his head and his heart are fruitless soil, and never would repay culture.

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: I beg pardon ; look at my hair and whiskers. (they all laugh) Oh, I don't mind being laughed at by such an exceedingly happy and fortunate gentleman as Mr. Hawthorne, or even by his friends, on the occasion of his wedding; but Dr. Playfair, I must object to being made the butt of your jests, after to-day.

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: Well, I don't object to that, for I hope never to see you after to day.

div.stage: Enter James, L.

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: Here comes James, to tender his resignation.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: Oh, no; I perceive there is going to be a coalition between the {h}opposition and {h}independent parties, and as my politics are very promiscuous, I beg to say, that I am willing to continue in {h}office, which his always the grand {h}object of a {h}upper.

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Ha! ha! ha!

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: And so the great Hawthorne property having been legitimately disposed of, and to everybody's satisfaction, ladies and gentlemen, I beg to inform you, that we have just thirty-five minutes, seven seconds, and a half left, to reach the church and get the marriage over: make haste; even a wedding can have no greater grace than punctuality.

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Oh, yes, yes!

sp.speaker: Mr. C.
sp.p: Indeed I shall give you one minute and a half, to name its rivals.

sp.speaker: Sir L.
sp.p: High connexions !

sp.speaker: Adol.
sp.p: Good investments !

sp.speaker: Everard.
sp.p: Honour and affection !

sp.speaker: Jenny.
sp.p: A little shrewdness !

sp.speaker: Mrs. W.
sp.p: No double dealings on either side !

sp.speaker: Miss E.
sp.p: Sentiment and simplicity !

sp.speaker: Robin.
sp.p: Ay ! wedding first, and dumplings after !

sp.speaker: Dr. P.
sp.p: A little objection to be got over.

sp.speaker: James.
sp.p: {H}office and perquisites to follow.

sp.speaker: Mrs. V.
sp.p: A great inheritance.

sp.speaker: Lucy.
sp.p: No ; the union of hearts with hands, (giving her hand to Frank)

sp.speaker: Frank.
sp.p: The truth lives not alone in one grace, one virtue, but in all; and as we are certain to lack some, to ensure happiness we should temper the conscious pride of our respective merits with a charitable consideration for their companion faults, and so preserve them from that dangerous affinity to vice, which virtue's self acquires when she runs into Extremes.

body.trailer: Printed by Thomas Scott, 1, Warwick Court, Holborn.
